1.title: Welcome to Auma
1.content: Auma is a constructed language designed to be completely transparent. Every letter is always pronounced the same way. Every sentence follows the same shape: Subject, Verb, Object. There are no conjugations, no irregular forms, no exceptions. If you can learn a handful of words, you can already build real sentences.

1.title: Five vowels, one rule
1.content: Auma uses five pure vowel sounds: a, e, i, o, u. Every written vowel is pronounced; vowel pairs are pronounced as their parts, not collapsed into English-style diphthongs. Stress falls on the next-to-last syllable of each root; compounds keep the sound of each part. What you see is what you say.

1.title: Your first verb
1.content: Now let's add a verb so these pronouns can do something. In Auma, the verb never changes form. 'esi' means am, is, and are — all in one word.

1.title: Building your first sentences
1.content: You already know enough to combine words. Auma sentences follow Subject + Verb order. Try reading these aloud.
1.aumaText: mi esi · tu esi · ta esi
1.englishText: I am · you are · they are

1.title: Subject + Verb + Descriptor
1.content: Add a descriptor after the verb to say something about the subject. The word order is always the same: who, then what they are, then the description.
1.aumaText: mi esi bona · tu esi bona · ta esi bona
1.englishText: I am good · you are good · they are good

1.title: Asking questions
1.content: To turn any statement into a yes/no question, place 'ka' at the beginning. The rest of the sentence stays exactly the same.
1.aumaText: tu esi bona → ka tu esi bona?
1.englishText: you are good → are you good?

1.title: Negation
1.content: Place 'no' directly before the verb to negate it. Questions and negation can combine: ka + subject + no + verb.
1.aumaText: mi no esi bona · ka tu no esi bona?
1.englishText: I am not good · are you not good?

1.title: Your first conversation
1.content: You now have eight words and three patterns: statements (mi esi bona), questions (ka tu esi bona?), and negation (mi no esi bona). That is enough to greet someone and have a short exchange.
1.aumaText: salama! ka tu esi bona? · mi esi bona. · ka ta esi bona? · ta no esi bona.
1.englishText: Hello! Are you good? · I am good. · Are they good? · They are not good.

1.question: How do you say 'they are good' in Auma?
1.explanation: Subject (ta) + verb (esi) + descriptor (bona). The order is always the same.

1.question: How do you turn 'mi esi bona' into a question?
1.explanation: Place 'ka' at the start of the sentence. Everything else stays in place.

1.question: How do you say 'you are not good'?
1.explanation: Place 'no' directly before the verb: tu no esi bona.

1.question: Build this sentence: 'Are they not good?'
1.explanation: Combine ka (question) + subject + no (negation) + verb + descriptor: ka ta no esi bona?

2.title: The System
2.content: Auma numbers follow one pattern with no exceptions. Learn ten base sounds, two structure words, and you can count to one hundred.
2.aumaText: nula · un · du · tri · fo · han · sisa · seti · soka · suna

2.title: Building tens
2.content: Place any base number before des to make tens. Two-ten is twenty. Five-ten is fifty. The pattern never changes.

2.title: Compound numbers
2.content: Add the units after the des. Twenty-three is du-des-tri. Forty-seven is fo-des-seti. Ninety-nine is suna-des-suna.

2.title: The full picture
2.content: You now have the complete number system. With just twelve words you can count from zero to hundreds. The pattern is always the same: digit + des for tens, then add the unit.

2.question: How do you say 23 in Auma?
How do you say 85 in Auma?
How do you say 71 in Auma?

3.title: The Self
3.content: You already know mi (I), tu (you), ta (they), and esi (am/is/are). Today you expand the circle — learning the plural pronouns, how to express what belongs to whom, and two new descriptors.
3.aumaText: mi esi bona — tu esi bona — now: mili, we, tuli, tali
3.englishText: I am good — you are good — now: we (excl.), we (incl.), you all, they all

3.title: The pronoun pattern
3.content: Notice the pattern: mi becomes mili, tu becomes tuli, ta becomes tali. The suffix -li marks a plural or collective group. The same regular plural logic will appear again when family and people words expand later. 'we' is special — it means everyone present, including the listener.
3.aumaText: mi · mili · we · tu · tuli · ta · tali
3.englishText: I · we (excl.) · we (incl.) · you · you all · they · they all

3.title: Possession with di
3.content: To show that something belongs to someone, place 'di' between the thing and the owner. The pattern is always: thing + di + owner. This never changes.
3.aumaText: salama di mi · salama di tu · salama di we
3.englishText: my peace · your peace · our peace (all of us)

3.title: Saying who you are
3.content: Now you can make full identity statements. Combine the possessive 'di' with 'esi' and your new descriptors to talk about yourself and your group.
3.aumaText: noma di mi esi Auma · we esi nova · ka tuli esi yuna?
3.englishText: my name is Auma · we are all new · are you all young?

3.title: Pointing with words
3.content: iti means 'this' (something close or present) and ita means 'that' (something further away or already mentioned). These are some of the most used words in any language — you will use them every day.
3.aumaText: iti esi bona · ita esi nova · ka iti esi di tu?
3.englishText: this is good · that is new · is this yours?

3.question: How do you say 'our peace' (everyone included)?
3.explanation: Use 'we' for inclusive we: salama di we. The pattern is thing + di + owner.

3.question: How do you say 'your peace' using the possessive?
3.explanation: Possession follows thing + di + owner: salama di tu.

3.question: How do you say 'my name is Nami'?
3.explanation: noma di mi (my name) + esi (is) + the name: noma di mi esi Nami.

3.question: How do you ask 'are they all young?'
3.explanation: Use ka at the start, tali for 'they (plural)', and yuna for 'young'.

4.title: The Feel
4.content: You can say who you are and describe yourself as bona, nova, or yuna. Today you go deeper — learning words for how you feel inside. These work exactly like the descriptors you already know: subject + esi + feeling.
4.aumaText: mi esi bona → mi esi hapi
4.englishText: I am good → I am happy

4.title: Feelings follow the same pattern
4.content: These emotion words slot into the exact same structure you learned on Day 1. Subject + esi + feeling. Questions and negation work the same way too.
4.aumaText: ka tu esi hapi? · mi no esi trisa
4.englishText: are you happy? · I am not sad

4.question: How do you say 'are you serene?' in Auma?
4.explanation: Questions use ka at the start: ka + subject + esi + descriptor.

4.title: Combining feelings with what you know
4.content: You can now build rich sentences about emotional states using all your pronouns and patterns. Negation, questions, plurals — they all work together.
4.aumaText: tali esi lasa · ka tuli esi hapi? · mi no esi ira
4.englishText: they are all tired · are you all happy? · I am not angry

4.title: esi vs. senti
4.content: Use esi to state what you are. Use senti to express what you feel. The difference is subtle — 'mi esi hapi' means 'I am happy' (a state). 'mi senti hapi' means 'I feel happy' (an experience). Both follow the same word order.
4.aumaText: mi esi kalma · mi senti kalma
4.englishText: I am calm · I feel calm

4.title: sereni vs. kalma
4.content: Auma has two words for calm states. 'sereni' means serene — a deep, luminous peace. 'kalma' means calm — a quieter, more everyday stillness. Both work the same way in sentences.
4.aumaText: mi esi sereni · mi esi kalma · mi senti sereni
4.englishText: I am serene · I am calm · I feel serene

4.question: How do you say 'they are not tired' in Auma?
4.explanation: Place 'no' before the verb: tali no esi lasa.

4.question: How do you say 'I feel calm'?
4.explanation: Use senti for feeling: mi senti kalma. 'mi esi kalma' would mean 'I am calm' — also valid, but the question asks about feeling.

4.question: How do you ask 'are you all sorry?'
4.explanation: Use tuli for 'you all' and ka at the start: ka tuli esi sori?

5.title: The Need
5.content: So far you can describe who you are and how you feel. Now Auma becomes useful — you learn to say what you want or need, how to ask for things politely, and words for food and drink. Same word order: subject + verb + object.

5.title: Verbs beyond esi
5.content: voli is your first desire verb. It follows the same subject + verb + object pattern. And just like esi, it never changes form — the same word works for I, you, they, everyone.
5.aumaText: mi voli · tu voli · tali voli
5.englishText: I want · you want · they all want

5.question: How do you say 'we all want water'?
5.explanation: we (we all, inclusive) + voli (want) + akwa (water). Subject + verb + object.

5.title: Negation with action verbs
5.content: Negation works exactly the same as before — place 'no' before the verb. This lets you refuse things or say what you do not want.
5.aumaText: mi no voli pano · tu no beva akwa
5.englishText: I do not want bread · you do not drink water

5.title: Commands vs requests
5.content: AUMA has two levels of directness. For a COMMAND (imperative), use the bare verb at the start: Kani! (Eat!) Beva akwa! (Drink water!) Dona pano! (Give bread!). For a polite REQUEST, add plisa: plisa, tu dona akwa (please, give water). Commands are direct and urgent. Requests are respectful. Both are correct — the choice shows your intention.
5.aumaText: kani! · beva akwa! · dona pano! · plisa, tu dona akwa
5.englishText: eat! · drink water! · give bread! · please, give water

5.title: Making polite requests
5.content: Combine plisa with a statement to make a request. In Auma, requests are just statements with plisa added. You can ask someone to give, to eat, or to drink.
5.aumaText: mi voli akwa, plisa · plisa, tu dona pano · plisa, tu dona akwa
5.englishText: I want water, please · please, give bread · please, give water

5.title: A complete exchange
5.content: You can now make requests and express thanks. With voli, dona, plisa, and gratao, you have a full polite exchange pattern.
5.aumaText: mi voli akwa, plisa. · tu dona akwa. · gratao!
5.englishText: I want water, please. · You give water. · Thank you!

5.question: How do you say 'please, give water'?
5.explanation: plisa at the start, then a normal statement: tu dona akwa. The fuller 'give water to me' form comes later after a = to is taught.

5.question: How do you say 'I do not want bread'?
5.explanation: Place 'no' before the verb: mi no voli pano.

5.question: How do you ask 'do you eat bread?'
5.explanation: ka at the start turns it into a question: ka tu kani pano?

5.question: Build this sentence: 'They do not drink water'
5.explanation: Subject (tali) + no + verb (beva) + object (akwa): tali no beva akwa.

6.title: The Day
6.content: You already know the numbers un through seti from Day 2. Auma day names are built directly from these numbers — no new roots to memorize. Just learn one new word, 'dina' (day), and combine it with what you already know.

6.title: Building the seven days
6.content: Each day of the week is simply dina + the number. Monday is dina-un (day one). Tuesday is dina-du (day two). The pattern continues all the way through Sunday, dina-seti (day seven). No exceptions.
6.aumaText: dina-un · dina-du · dina-tri · dina-fo · dina-han · dina-sisa · dina-seti
6.englishText: Monday · Tuesday · Wednesday · Thursday · Friday · Saturday · Sunday

6.title: Days connect to numbers
6.content: You already know un, du, tri, fo, han, sisa, seti. Adding dina- in front turns them into days of the week. This is the same compound pattern you used for tens: du-des (20), now dina-du (Tuesday).

6.question: How do you say 'Thursday is good' in Auma?
6.explanation: Thursday is dina-fo (day four). dina-fo esi bona.

6.sentenceTranslation: I feel good in the morning
I am tired in the evening

6.title: Time words in sentences
6.content: Time words can go at the start or end of a sentence. Both positions mean the same thing. You can layer time words with everything from Week 1 — feelings, questions, negation, pronouns.
6.aumaText: mi esi hapi hodi · matina mi kani pano · ka tu esi lasa sera?
6.englishText: I am happy today · in the morning I eat bread · are you tired in the evening?

6.title: Talking about your week
6.content: You can now combine day names with everything you have learned — feelings, questions, negation, pronouns. Describe your week in full sentences.
6.aumaText: mi esi hapi dina-un · mi esi lasa dina-han · ka tu esi bona dina-seti?
6.englishText: I am happy on Monday · I am tired on Friday · are you good on Sunday?

6.question: How do you say 'we are all serene on Sunday'?
6.explanation: we (we all, inclusive) + esi sereni (are serene) + dina-seti (Sunday).

6.question: What day is dina-han?
6.explanation: han is five, so dina-han is day five — Friday.

6.question: How do you say 'tomorrow I want bread, please'?
6.explanation: doma (tomorrow) + mi voli pano (I want bread) + plisa (please): doma mi voli pano, plisa.

7.title: Hello, World
7.content: You have spent six days building the inner architecture of Auma — pronouns, numbers, feelings, needs, time. Now the path turns outward. Today you learn the social words that let you greet, connect, and say goodbye. You already know salama (peace/hello) and noma (name). Now you complete the picture.

7.title: Introductions
7.content: You learned noma (name) and di (of/possessive) on Day 3. Combine them to introduce yourself. The pattern is: noma di mi esi [your name]. To ask a yes/no name question, use ka with a guessed name: ka noma di tu esi Nami? The open 'what is your name?' form uses kosa later on Day 29.
7.aumaText: noma di mi esi Nami · ka noma di tu esi Nami?
7.englishText: my name is Nami · is your name Nami?

7.question: How do you say 'my name is Kano'?
7.explanation: noma di mi (my name) + esi (is) + the name: noma di mi esi Kano.

7.title: The language of light
7.content: Auma means 'dawn' — the first light. With seven days of vocabulary, you can introduce yourself, express feelings, ask for what you need, talk about your week, and connect with others. Every sentence follows the same transparent pattern. The foundation is built.
7.aumaText: mi esi bona. mili esi bona. gratao, ami.
7.englishText: I am good. We are good. Thank you, friend.

7.question: How do you say 'you are my friend' in Auma?
7.explanation: tu (you) + esi (are) + ami di mi (friend of me / my friend).

7.question: How would you greet someone and introduce yourself as Nami?
options: salama! noma di mi esi Nami.
7.explanation: salama greets, then noma di mi esi Nami gives the introduction.

7.question: How do you say 'thank you, I feel happy'?
7.explanation: gratao (thank you), then mi senti hapi (I feel happy). senti is for feeling, esi is for being.

7.title: A preview: how you know what you know
7.content: Most languages let you say 'the sky is blue.' AUMA is different — it lets you mark HOW you know something. Did you see it yourself? Hear it from someone? Feel it intuitively? Work it out logically? You will learn four special markers later (nuna, odi, intu, padi), but for now just know: AUMA cares not just about what you say, but how you know it. This is called evidentiality, and it is one of the most unique features of this language.

8.title: The Place
8.content: You can say who you are, how you feel, and what you want. Now you learn where things are. Auma uses two small prepositions that never change — and six place words to give them meaning. Same sentence shape: subject + verb + preposition + place.
8.aumaText: mi esi ... → mi esi en tomo
8.englishText: I am ... → I am in the house

8.title: Two prepositions, big power
8.content: en tells you where something already is. a tells you where something is going. Both come before the destination. These never change form — they work the same way in every sentence.
8.aumaText: mi esi en ... · mi voli anda a ...
8.englishText: I am at ... · I want to go to ...

8.sentenceTranslation: I want to go to the market

8.title: Combining prepositions with places
8.content: Use en + place to say where you are. Use voli + anda + a + place to say where you want to go. You can combine these with voli (want) and dona (give) from earlier days to make useful requests.
8.aumaText: mi esi en tomo · mi voli anda a merkato · plisa, tu beni a tomo
8.englishText: I am in the house · I want to go to the market · please, come to the house

8.sentenceTranslation: they are all in the park

8.question: How do you say 'I am in the house'?
8.explanation: Use 'en' for being at a location: mi esi en tomo.

8.title: Questions about place
8.content: Remember ka from Day 1? You can ask about locations the same way. And you can use the possessive di from Day 3 with places too.
8.aumaText: ka tu esi en tomo? · tomo di mi · ka kafe esi bona?
8.englishText: are you in the house? · my house · is the cafe good?

8.question: How do you say 'I want to go to the market'?
8.explanation: Use voli + anda + a + destination: mi voli anda a merkato.

8.question: How do you ask 'are you at the cafe?'
8.explanation: ka at the start for a question. en for being at a location: ka tu esi en kafe?

8.question: How do you say 'the temple is in the park'?
8.explanation: Use 'en' for location: templo esi en parko.

9.title: The Time
9.content: You know the days of the week from Day 6 — dina-un through dina-seti. You know hodi (today), doma (tomorrow), matina (morning), and sera (evening). Now you learn more time words to say when things happen. Time words are placed at the start or end of a sentence.
9.aumaText: mi esi hapi hodi → mi esi hapi nau
9.englishText: I am happy today → I am happy now

9.sentenceTranslation: I am in the house now
before, I was at the market

9.sentence: pos, mi voli anda a kafe
9.sentenceTranslation: later, I want to go to the cafe

9.title: Time placement
9.content: Time words can go at the start or end of a sentence. 'nau mi esi en tomo' and 'mi esi en tomo nau' both mean the same thing. This mirrors how you used day names on Day 6.
9.aumaText: nau mi esi en tomo · mi esi en tomo nau
9.englishText: now I am in the house · I am in the house now

9.sentenceTranslation: yesterday I was in the park
I am in the house at night

9.question: How do you say 'I am at the cafe now'?
9.explanation: Same pattern — place the time word at the end: mi esi en kafe nau.

9.sentenceTranslation: I am already in the house

9.title: Building time sentences with what you know
9.content: You can now layer time with everything from Week 1 — feelings, places, wants, questions. The sentence pattern stays the same; you just add when it happens. Time adjuncts like hodi and yesta can go at the start or end. Frequency words like sempre and neva go directly before the verb. Later tense/aspect particles also form their own class.
9.aumaText: hodi mi senti hapi · ka tu esi en tomo nau? · yesta mi esi en merkato · mi sempre voli anda a parko
9.englishText: today I feel happy · are you in the house now? · yesterday I was at the market · I always want to go to the park

9.question: How do you say 'yesterday I was at the temple'?
9.explanation: Time word at the start: yesta mi esi en templo. Both options with yesta at start or end are valid.

9.question: How do you say 'I never want water'?
9.explanation: Place neva before the verb: mi neva voli akwa.

9.question: How do you say 'we are already in the park'?
9.explanation: Place deja before the verb: mili deja esi en parko.

10.title: The Action
10.content: You already know several verbs: esi (am/is/are), senti (feel), voli (want), dona (give), beni (come), kani (eat), beva (drink), parla (speak), and anda (walk/go). Today you add more verbs of daily life. Remember — Auma verbs never change form. The same word works for every person.
10.aumaText: mi voli → mi domi · mi kani → mi koka
10.englishText: I want → I sleep · I eat → I cook

10.sentenceTranslation: I sleep in the house at night

10.title: Walk and sleep with places and time
10.content: Combine anda and domi with prepositions from Day 8 and time words from Day 9. Use 'a' for where you go and 'en' for where you already are. Add time at the start or end.
10.aumaText: mi anda a parko matina · mi domi en tomo noka · tu anda a kafe pos
10.englishText: I walk to the park in the morning · I sleep in the house at night · you go to the cafe later

10.question: How do you say 'they walk to the market'?
10.explanation: Subject (ta) + verb (anda) + direction (a merkato). Use 'a' for going to a place.

10.title: A daily routine
10.content: With these verbs you can describe an entire day. Add the time words from Day 9 to say when each thing happens.
10.aumaText: matina mi lava · mi kani pano · mi beva akwa · mi anda a merkato · sera mi legi · noka mi domi
10.englishText: in the morning I wash · I eat bread · I drink water · I walk to the market · in the evening I read · at night I sleep

10.title: Negation and questions with new verbs
10.content: These verbs follow every rule you already know. Place 'no' before the verb to negate. Place 'ka' at the start to ask. Nothing new to memorize — just new verbs in the same patterns.
10.aumaText: mi no domi · ka tu anda a tomo? · mi no voli koka
10.englishText: I do not sleep · are you going home? · I do not want to cook

10.question: How do you say 'we all read in the evening'?
10.explanation: we is the inclusive 'we all'; mili means 'we, not you.' So the correct sentence is we legi sera.

10.question: How do you say 'I do not want to walk to the market'?
options: mi no voli anda a merkato
mi voli no anda a merkato
no mi voli anda a merkato
10.explanation: Place 'no' before the first verb: mi no voli anda a merkato.

10.question: How do you ask 'do they cook at the house?'
10.explanation: ka at the start, then the statement: ka tali koka en tomo?

11.title: The Link
11.content: You can build many sentences now — but they stand alone. Today you learn the words that link thoughts together. Connectors let you combine ideas, give reasons, and express choices. These small words unlock complex expression.
11.aumaText: mi esi hapi · tu esi hapi → mi esi hapi e tu esi hapi
11.englishText: I am happy · you are happy → I am happy and you are happy

11.title: Linking nouns and linking sentences
11.content: 'e' and 'o' can connect single words or entire sentences. When connecting nouns, they go between the nouns. When connecting sentences, they go between the two complete thoughts.
11.aumaText: akwa e pano · mi anda a tomo e tu anda a merkato
11.englishText: water and bread · I go home and you go to the market

11.sentence: mi esi lasa pero mi esi hapi
11.sentenceTranslation: I am tired but I am happy

11.question: How do you say 'I want water and bread'?
11.explanation: 'e' means and. mi voli akwa e pano = I want water and bread.

11.sentence: mi esi hapi porke tu esi en tomo
11.sentenceTranslation: I am happy because you are in the house

11.title: Giving reasons with porke
11.content: porke means 'because' and connects a reason to a statement. For asking 'why' in questions, AUMA has a dedicated word keno (taught Day 29). For now, porke can also start a question to ask why — but once you learn keno, use keno for questions and porke for reasons.
11.aumaText: porke tu esi trisa? · mi esi trisa porke mi esi lasa
11.englishText: why are you sad? · I am sad because I am tired

11.sentence: si tu esi lasa, tu domi
11.sentenceTranslation: if you are tired, you sleep

11.sentence: mi senti ke tu esi bona
11.sentenceTranslation: I feel that you are good

11.title: ke for linking ideas and describing things
11.content: ke works two ways. As a connector: mi senti ke tu esi bona (I feel THAT you are good). As a relative pronoun: ami ke dona pano (the friend WHO gave bread). In both cases, ke links a main idea to a dependent clause. This is one of the most powerful words in AUMA.
11.aumaText: mi senti ke tu esi bona · ami ke dona pano esi bona · tomo ke esi granda esi di mi
11.englishText: I feel that you are good · the friend who gave bread is good · the house that is big is mine

11.title: Complex thoughts from simple pieces
11.content: With just seven linking words, you can express reasons, conditions, contrasts, and additions. Every sentence still follows the same word order — the connector simply bridges two thoughts together.
11.aumaText: si mi lava matina, mi kani e beva akwa · mi anda a merkato pero mi no esi lasa porke mi senti kalma
11.englishText: if I wash in the morning, I eat and drink water · I walk to the market but I am not tired because I feel calm

11.question: How do you say 'I am tired but I am not sad'?
options: mi esi lasa pero mi no esi trisa
mi esi lasa e mi no esi trisa
mi esi lasa porke mi no esi trisa
11.explanation: 'pero' means but — it connects a contrast: mi esi lasa pero mi no esi trisa.

11.question: How do you ask 'why are you not in the house?'
options: porke tu no esi en tomo?
ka tu no esi en tomo porke?
porke no tu esi en tomo?
11.explanation: porke at the start asks why: porke tu no esi en tomo?

11.question: How do you say 'if you are sad, I am also sad'?
options: si tu esi trisa, mi anke esi trisa
si tu esi trisa, mi esi trisa
porke tu esi trisa, mi anke esi trisa
11.explanation: si (if) starts the condition, anke means also: si tu esi trisa, mi anke esi trisa.

12.title: The World
12.content: You have been describing yourself, your feelings, and the places you go. Today the view opens wide — you learn the words for the natural world around you. These are nouns, and they work exactly like tomo and merkato. Use them with esi, en, a, and di.
12.aumaText: mi esi en tomo → luna esi en sielo
12.englishText: I am in the house → the moon is in the sky

12.sentenceTranslation: the moon is in the sky

12.title: Nature follows the same patterns
12.content: Nature words slot into every pattern you already know. Use en for where they are. Use di for what belongs to whom. Use ka to ask about them. The grammar never changes.
12.aumaText: luna esi en sielo · ka stela esi en sielo? · tera di mili
12.englishText: the moon is in the sky · are the stars in the sky? · our earth

12.sentenceTranslation: the stars are in the sky at night
the tree is in the park

12.question: How do you say 'the moon is in the sky'?
12.explanation: Use 'en' for location: luna esi en sielo.

12.title: Describing the world around you
12.content: Combine nature words with time, feelings, connectors, and everything you know. You can describe what you see, where you walk, and how the world makes you feel.
12.aumaText: mi anda a monta matina · mi senti kalma en mara · arbo esi en tera e stela esi en sielo
12.englishText: I walk to the mountain in the morning · I feel calm at the sea · the tree is on the earth and the star is in the sky

12.question: How do you say 'I walk to the river'?
12.explanation: Use 'a' for direction: mi anda a rivo.

12.question: How do you say 'the stars are in the sky at night'?
12.explanation: stela (stars) + esi en sielo (are in the sky) + noka (at night).

12.question: How do you say 'I feel calm because the sea is good'?
options: mi senti kalma porke mara esi bona
mi senti kalma e mara esi bona
12.explanation: Use porke (because) to link the reason: mi senti kalma porke mara esi bona.

13.title: The Body
13.content: You can talk about the world, places, time, and feelings. Now you learn the words for your own body. Body parts are nouns — they combine with di (possessive) and esi just like everything else you know. Today you also learn a pain word and a healing verb.
13.aumaText: kapo di mi · mano di tu · kora di mili
13.englishText: my head · your hand · our heart

13.title: Body parts with di
13.content: Use the possessive di from Day 3 to say whose body part it is. The pattern is always: body part + di + person. You already know this — it works just like tomo di mi (my house) or noma di tu (your name).
13.aumaText: kapo di mi · mano di tu · kora di ta
13.englishText: my head · your hand · their heart

13.question: How do you say 'my heart is calm'?
13.explanation: Use the possessive pattern: kora di mi (heart of me) + esi kalma (is calm).

13.sentence: mi parla e boka di mi esi bona
13.sentenceTranslation: I speak and my mouth is good
I feel pain in my head

13.title: Describing how your body feels
13.content: Combine body parts with senti (feel) and dolo (pain) to describe physical sensations. Use sana (heal) to talk about recovery. All the same patterns apply — negation, questions, possessives, time.
13.aumaText: mi senti dolo en kapo · ka tu senti dolo en mano? · tu sana mi · pos, mi no senti dolo
13.englishText: I feel pain in my head · do you feel pain in your hand? · you heal me · later, I do not feel pain

13.title: Putting it together with the body
13.content: You can now describe your body, say where it hurts, and talk about healing — using everything from both weeks.
13.aumaText: mi senti dolo en korpo pero mi voli sana · kora di mi esi kalma porke mi esi en templo
13.englishText: I feel pain in my body but I want to heal · my heart is calm because I am in the temple

13.question: How do you say 'I feel pain in my head'?
13.explanation: mi senti (I feel) + dolo (pain) + en kapo (in the head).

13.question: How do you ask 'does your body feel good?'
options: ka korpo di tu esi bona?
korpo di tu ka esi bona?
13.explanation: ka at the start, then korpo di tu (your body) + esi bona (is good).

13.question: How do you say 'I feel pain but I always want to heal'?
options: mi senti dolo pero mi sempre voli sana
mi senti dolo e mi sempre voli sana
mi dolo pero mi sana sempre
13.explanation: pero connects the contrast: mi senti dolo pero mi sempre voli sana.

14.title: The Review
14.content: Week 2 is complete. You started Week 1 with pronouns and basic sentences. Now you can talk about places, time, daily actions, give reasons, describe the natural world, and name parts of your body. This review tests your ability to combine everything. No new words — only new combinations of what you already know.
14.aumaText: mi esi kalma. mili esi bona. ka tu esi kalma?
14.englishText: I am calm. We are good. Are you calm?

14.title: Week 2 vocabulary map
14.content: Day 8: en, a, tomo, merkato, parko, via, kafe, templo. Day 9: nau, ante, pos, yesta, noka, sempre, neva, ya (yes), deja (already). Day 10: anda, domi, lava, koka, skirvi, legi, toka (touch), vela. Day 11: e, o, pero, porke, si, ke, anke. Day 12: tera, sielo, luna, stela, arbo, mara, rivo, monta. Day 13: korpo, kapo, kora, mano, oki, boka, dolo, sana.

14.title: Reading passage — a full day
14.content: Read this passage aloud. Every word comes from Days 1-13. This is what two weeks of Auma can do.
14.aumaText: matina mi lava e mi kani pano e beva akwa. mi anda a merkato en via. mi vela luna e stela en sielo ante. nau mi esi en kafe. sera mi legi e skirvi en tomo. mi senti kalma porke kora di mi esi bona. noka mi domi. salama.
14.englishText: In the morning I wash and I eat bread and drink water. I walk to the market on the road. I saw the moon and stars in the sky before. Now I am in the cafe. In the evening I read and write in the house. I feel calm because my heart is good. At night I sleep. Peace.

14.title: Reading passage — the world
14.content: Read this second passage. It combines nature, body, and emotion vocabulary from Week 2.
14.aumaText: mi anda a monta e mi vela mara. arbo esi en tera. stela esi en sielo noka. mi senti dolo en kapo pero mi voli sana. mi senti ke tera esi bona. mi sempre ama tera e sielo e mara.
14.englishText: I walk to the mountain and I see the sea. The tree is on the earth. The stars are in the sky at night. I feel pain in my head but I want to heal. I feel that the earth is good. I always love the earth and sky and sea.

14.question: How do you say 'I walk to the market in the morning'?
14.explanation: mi (I) + anda (walk) + a merkato (to the market) + matina (in the morning). Use 'a' for direction.

14.question: How do you say 'I cook in the house and I eat bread'?
options: mi koka en tomo e mi kani pano
mi koka en tomo o mi kani pano
mi koka e kani pano en tomo
14.explanation: Two complete thoughts linked with 'e' (and): mi koka en tomo e mi kani pano.

14.question: How do you say 'the stars are in the sky but the moon is not in the sky'?
options: stela esi en sielo pero luna no esi en sielo
stela esi en sielo e luna no esi en sielo
stela en sielo pero luna no en sielo esi
14.explanation: Use 'pero' to contrast: stela esi en sielo pero luna no esi en sielo.

14.question: How do you say 'I feel pain in my body because I always walk to the mountain'?
options: mi senti dolo en korpo porke mi sempre anda a monta
mi senti dolo en korpo e mi sempre anda a monta
mi dolo korpo porke mi anda monta sempre
14.explanation: porke links the reason: mi senti dolo en korpo porke mi sempre anda a monta.

14.question: How do you say 'if you feel sad, I also feel sad'?
options: si tu senti trisa, mi anke senti trisa
porke tu senti trisa, mi anke senti trisa
si tu esi trisa, mi esi trisa
14.explanation: si (if) starts the condition, anke (also): si tu senti trisa, mi anke senti trisa.

14.question: Translate this sentence: 'yesta mi anda a templo e mi senti kalma en kora'
options: yesterday I walked to the temple and I felt calm in my heart
yesterday I walked to the temple but I felt calm in my heart
before I walked to the temple and I felt pain in my heart
14.explanation: yesta (yesterday) + mi anda a templo (I walked to the temple) + e (and) + mi senti kalma en kora (I felt calm in my heart).

14.question: How do you say 'I never sleep in the park at night'?
options: mi neva domi en parko noka
mi no domi en parko noka
14.explanation: neva (never) before the verb: mi neva domi en parko noka.

14.title: A conversation at the cafe
14.content: Read this dialogue aloud. Two friends meet. Every word comes from Days 1-13.
14.aumaText:
— salama, ami! ka tu esi bona?
— mi esi bona, gratao. mi deja kani pano e beva akwa. ka tu voli kani?
— mi no voli kani nau. mi anda a templo pos.
— bona! mi anke voli anda a templo. mi senti ke templo esi kalma.
— mi senti kalma anke. mi ama templo porke kora di mi esi sereni en templo.
— adio, ami. salama!
14.englishText:
— Hello, friend! Are you good?
— I am good, thanks. I already ate bread and drank water. Do you want to eat?
— I do not want to eat now. I walk to the temple later.
— Good! I also want to walk to the temple. I feel that the temple is calm.
— I feel calm also. I love the temple because my heart is serene in the temple.
— Goodbye, friend. Peace!

14.title: Describing your world
14.content: Use everything you know to describe the world around you. Combine places, nature, body, and time into one scene.
14.aumaText: matina mi lava e mi anda a via. mi vela arbo en tera e stela en sielo. mi anda a mara. mara esi kalma. mi senti bona en korpo. oki di mi vela luna. kora di mi esi sereni. mi sempre ama tera.
14.englishText: In the morning I wash and I walk on the road. I see trees on the earth and stars in the sky. I walk to the sea. The sea is calm. I feel good in my body. My eyes see the moon. My heart is serene. I always love the earth.

14.title: Two weeks complete
14.content: You now know over 100 Auma words and can build sentences about identity, feelings, wants, days, greetings, places, time, actions, reasons, nature, and the body. The grammar has not changed once — Subject + Verb + Object, ka for questions, no for negation, di for possession. The foundation is strong. Week 3 begins tomorrow.
14.aumaText: gratao. mi esi kalma e bona. kora di mi esi bona. adio, ami.
14.englishText: Thank you. I am calm and good. My heart is good. Goodbye, friend.

15.title: The Food
15.content: You already know kani (eat), beva (drink), akwa (water), and pano (bread). Today you learn eight more food words — drinks, ingredients, and meals. Use plisa and voli to order and request food.
15.aumaText: mi kani pano · mi beva akwa · mi voli fruta
15.englishText: I eat bread · I drink water · I want fruit

15.title: Drinks
15.content: Auma has simple words for common drinks. Combine them with beva (drink) and voli (want) to make requests.
15.aumaText: mi beva kopi · mi beva teo · mi beva suko
15.englishText: I drink coffee · I drink tea · I drink juice

15.sentenceTranslation: I drink coffee in the morning
do you want tea or coffee?
I drink milk in the evening

15.question: How do you say 'I drink tea in the morning'?
15.explanation: Subject + verb + object + time: mi beva teo en matina.

15.title: Solid foods
15.content: Now add some foods to your vocabulary. These combine with kani (eat) and koka (cook) just like pano.
15.aumaText: mi kani fruta · mi koka karni · mi kani ovo
15.englishText: I eat fruit · I cook meat · I eat eggs

15.sentenceTranslation: I cook meat in the evening
I eat eggs in the morning

15.title: Ordering and requesting food
15.content: Use plisa (please) and voli (want) to make polite requests. You can order at a kafe or merkato.
15.aumaText: mi voli sopa, plisa · ka tu voli kopi o teo? · mi voli ovo e pano, plisa
15.englishText: I want soup, please · do you want coffee or tea? · I want eggs and bread, please

15.question: How do you say 'I want milk, please'?
How do you say 'I cook soup in the evening'?
15.explanation: Subject + verb + object + time: mi koka sopa en sera.

15.question: How do you ask 'do you want fruit or meat?'
options: ka tu voli fruta o karni?
tu voli fruta o karni ka?
ka fruta o karni tu voli?
15.explanation: ka at the start, then the statement: ka tu voli fruta o karni?

16.title: The Color
16.content: Today you learn colors. In Auma, colors work as descriptors — they come after esi just like bona or nova. You can describe anything you already know: sielo esi blu, arbo esi verda.
16.aumaText: sielo esi blu · arbo esi verda · stela esi dora
16.englishText: the sky is blue · the tree is green · the star is golden

16.title: Colors with nature
16.content: Combine your colors with nature words from Day 12. Predicate color uses noun + esi + color: mara esi blu. Attributive color uses noun + color: mara blu means blue sea. Both patterns are regular.
16.aumaText: mara esi blu · tera esi verda · luna esi blanka
16.englishText: the sea is blue · the earth is green · the moon is white

16.question: How do you say 'the sky is blue'?
16.explanation: Noun + esi + descriptor: sielo esi blu.

16.title: Combining colors with questions and negation
16.content: You already know ka (question) and no (negation). Use them with colors exactly as before.
16.aumaText: ka sielo esi blu? · sielo no esi roja · ka arbo esi verda? · arbo no esi negra
16.englishText: is the sky blue? · the sky is not red · is the tree green? · the tree is not black

16.question: How do you say 'the moon is not red'?
16.explanation: Subject + no + esi + descriptor: luna no esi roja.

16.question: How do you ask 'is the star golden?'
16.explanation: ka at the start: ka stela esi dora?

16.question: How do you say 'the sea is blue and the earth is green'?
options: mara esi blu e tera esi verda
mara blu e tera verda esi
16.explanation: Two statements joined with e: mara esi blu e tera esi verda.

17.title: The Kin
17.content: You know ami (friend) and ama (love). Today you learn family words. Use di (possessive) to say whose family member: mama di mi means my mother, papa di tu means your father.
17.aumaText: mama di mi · papa di tu · sina di ta
17.englishText: my mother · your father · their child

17.sentence: papa di tu esi en tomo

17.title: Possessives with di
17.content: The pattern is always: noun + di + person. You already know this from Day 3 — it works for all family words too.
17.aumaText: sibra di mi · mama di tu · papa di ta · sina di we
17.englishText: my sibling · your mother · their father · our child

17.sentence: sibra di mi esi ami di mi
marito di mi esi en merkato
17.sentenceTranslation: my husband is at the market

17.question: How do you say 'my mother is good'?
17.explanation: Noun + di + owner: mama di mi esi bona.

17.sentence: tana di we esi en templo
17.sentenceTranslation: our elder is at the temple

17.title: Family sentences
17.content: Combine family words with everything you know — places, feelings, questions, and negation.
17.aumaText: ka mama di tu esi en tomo? · papa di mi no esi lasa · amili di we esi bona
17.englishText: is your mother at home? · my father is not tired · our family is good

17.question: How do you say 'their child is young'?
17.explanation: sina di ta (their child) + esi yuna (is young).

17.question: How do you ask 'is your sibling at the park?'
options: ka sibra di tu esi en parko?
sibra di tu esi en parko ka?
ka sibra tu di en parko?
17.explanation: ka at the start: ka sibra di tu esi en parko?

17.question: How do you say 'my family is happy and my heart is calm'?
options: amili di mi esi hapi e kora di mi esi kalma
mi amili esi hapi e mi kora esi kalma
amili di mi hapi e kora di mi kalma
17.explanation: Two possessive statements joined with e.

18.title: The Weather
18.content: You know sielo (sky), luna (moon), and stela (star). Today you learn to describe the weather. Weather words work as descriptors with esi, just like colors: hodi esi ventosa means today is windy.
18.aumaText: hodi esi ventosa · dina esi kalda · noka esi frida
18.englishText: today is windy · the day is hot · the night is cold

18.sentenceTranslation: the day is sunny and hot

18.title: Describing the day
18.content: Use hodi (today), dina (day), matina (morning), and sera (evening) with weather words. The pattern is always: time + esi + weather descriptor.
18.aumaText: matina esi nubosa · sera esi frida · hodi no esi solosa
18.englishText: the morning is cloudy · the evening is cold · today is not sunny

18.question: How do you say 'today is cloudy'?
18.explanation: Time + esi + descriptor: hodi esi nubosa.

18.title: Weather questions and negation
18.content: Use ka and no with weather words the same way you always have.
18.aumaText: ka hodi esi frida? · hodi no esi ventosa · ka klima esi bona? · klima no esi kalda
18.englishText: is today cold? · today is not windy · is the weather good? · the weather is not hot

18.question: How do you ask 'is the morning cold?'
18.explanation: ka at the start: ka matina esi frida?

18.question: How do you say 'the sky is cloudy but the weather is good'?
options: sielo esi nubosa pero klima esi bona
18.explanation: Two statements joined with pero: sielo esi nubosa pero klima esi bona.

18.question: How do you say 'the earth is dry and the day is hot'?
options: tera esi seka e dina esi kalda
tera seka e dina kalda esi
18.explanation: Two statements joined with e: tera esi seka e dina esi kalda.

19.title: The Movement
19.content: You know anda (walk), domi (sleep), lava (wash), koka (cook), and other daily verbs. Today you learn verbs of motion and thought. They follow the same pattern: mi + verb, or mi + verb + en + place.
19.aumaText: mi kuri · mi nada · mi salta · mi pensa
19.englishText: I run · I swim · I jump · I think

19.sentence: sina di mi salta en parko
19.sentenceTranslation: my child jumps in the park

19.title: Movement with places
19.content: Combine movement verbs with the places you learned in Day 8: parko, merkato, via, tomo. The pattern is always: subject + verb + en + place.
19.aumaText: mi kuri en via · tu nada en rivo · ta salta en parko
19.englishText: I run on the road · you swim in the river · they jump in the park

19.question: How do you say 'I run in the park'?
19.explanation: Subject + verb + en + place: mi kuri en parko.

19.sentence: mi teni mano di sina di mi
mi pensa e mi senti kalma
19.sentenceTranslation: I think and I feel calm

19.title: Combining movement with feelings
19.content: Use e (and) and porke (because) to connect actions with feelings, just like you learned in Day 11.
19.aumaText: mi kuri porke mi senti hapi · mi nada e mi senti kalma · mi pensa porke mi voli senti sereni
19.englishText: I run because I feel happy · I swim and I feel calm · I think because I want to feel serene

19.question: How do you say 'I swim in the river'?
19.explanation: Subject + verb + en + place: mi nada en rivo.

19.question: How do you say 'they carry bread to the market'?
19.explanation: Subject + verb + object + a + destination: ta porta pano a merkato.

19.question: How do you say 'I think and I feel calm'?
options: mi pensa e mi senti kalma
mi pensa mi senti kalma e
pensa mi e kalma senti mi
19.explanation: Two statements joined with e: mi pensa e mi senti kalma.

20.title: The Feel Deep
20.content: On Day 4 you learned basic emotions: hapi, trisa, sereni, ira, lasa, sori, kalma. Today you go deeper — love, hope, freedom, and more. These work the same way with senti and esi.
20.aumaText: mi senti amora · mi senti libre · mi esi pronta
20.englishText: I feel love · I feel free · I am ready

20.sentence: mi senti amora en kora di mi
20.sentenceTranslation: I feel love in my heart

20.title: Deeper feelings with senti
20.content: Use senti (feel) with these deeper emotions. You can add porke (because) to explain why you feel that way.
20.aumaText: mi senti libre porke mi kuri en parko · mi senti amora porke amili di mi esi bona
20.englishText: I feel free because I run in the park · I feel love because my family is good

20.sentenceTranslation: I feel free on the mountain

20.question: How do you say 'I feel love in my heart'?
options: mi senti amora en kora di mi
mi amora senti en kora mi
senti mi amora kora di mi
20.explanation: mi senti amora (I feel love) + en kora di mi (in my heart).

20.sentence: mi no senti sola porke ami di mi esi en tomo
20.sentenceTranslation: I do not feel alone because my friend is at home

20.title: Emotion conversations
20.content: Combine deep emotions with questions and negation. Use everything you know to express complex feelings.
20.aumaText: ka tu senti libre? · mi no senti sola · mi senti kontenta e pronta · ka ta senti spera?
20.englishText: do you feel free? · I do not feel alone · I feel content and ready · do they feel hope?

20.question: How do you say 'I feel free on the mountain'?
20.explanation: mi senti libre (I feel free) + en monta (on the mountain).

20.question: How do you say 'I do not feel frustrated'?
20.explanation: Subject + no + verb + emotion: mi no senti frustra.

20.question: How do you ask 'are you ready?'
20.explanation: ka at the start: ka tu esi pronta?

21.title: Week 3 Review
21.content: You have completed three weeks of Auma. You now know food, colors, family, weather, movement, and deep emotions. This review tests everything from Weeks 1 through 3.

21.title: Your vocabulary so far
21.content: Week 1 gave you core structure: pronouns, numbers, possessives, emotions, requests, days, and greetings. Week 2 gave you navigation: places, time, daily verbs, connectors, nature, and body. Week 3 gave you depth: food, colors, family, weather, movement, and deeper feelings.
21.aumaText: mi voli kopi, plisa · sielo esi blu · mama di mi esi bona · hodi esi solosa · mi kuri en parko · mi senti libre
21.englishText: I want coffee, please · the sky is blue · my mother is good · today is sunny · I run in the park · I feel free

21.question: How do you say 'my family is happy'?
21.explanation: amili di mi (my family) + esi hapi (is happy).

21.question: How do you say 'I drink tea in the morning and I eat fruit'?
options: mi beva teo en matina e mi kani fruta
mi teo beva matina e mi fruta kani
beva mi teo matina e kani mi fruta
21.explanation: Two statements joined with e: mi beva teo en matina e mi kani fruta.

21.question: How do you say 'the star is golden and the moon is white'?
options: stela esi dora e luna esi blanka
stela dora esi e luna blanka esi
21.explanation: Two color statements joined with e: stela esi dora e luna esi blanka.

21.question: How do you say 'today is windy but the weather is good'?
options: hodi esi ventosa pero klima esi bona
21.explanation: Two weather statements joined with pero: hodi esi ventosa pero klima esi bona.

21.question: How do you say 'I run because I feel free'?
options: mi kuri porke mi senti libre
mi kuri mi senti libre porke
porke mi kuri mi senti libre
21.explanation: Action + porke + reason: mi kuri porke mi senti libre.

21.question: How do you say 'my child swims in the river'?
options: sina di mi nada en rivo
21.explanation: sina di mi (my child) + nada (swims) + en rivo (in the river).

21.question: How do you ask 'is your husband at the market?'
options: ka marito di tu esi en merkato?
marito di tu esi en merkato ka?
ka marito tu di en merkato?
21.explanation: ka at the start: ka marito di tu esi en merkato?

21.title: Reading passage
21.content: Read this passage aloud. Every word is one you have learned.
21.aumaText: salama! mi esi hapi hodi. klima esi solosa e kalda. mi kuri en parko en matina. mi beva suko pos. mi senti libre e kontenta. amili di mi esi en tomo. mama di mi koka sopa e papa di mi legi en sera. mi ama amili di mi. gratao.
21.englishText: Hello! I am happy today. The weather is sunny and warm. I run in the park in the morning. I drink juice after. I feel free and content. My family is at home. My mother cooks soup and my father reads in the evening. I love my family. Thank you.

21.title: Conversation practice
21.content: Practice this conversation between two friends.
21.aumaText:
A: salama, ami! ka tu esi bona?
B: mi esi kontenta. klima esi solosa hodi!
A: ya! mi kuri en parko en matina. ka tu voli kopi?
B: mi voli teo, plisa. mi no beva kopi.
A: bona. ka amili di tu esi bona?
B: ya, gratao. mama di mi esi hapi. e tu?
A: mi senti spera e libre. adio, ami!
B: adio!
21.englishText:
A: Hello, friend! Are you good?
B: I am content. The weather is sunny today!
A: Yes! I run in the park in the morning. Do you want coffee?
B: I want tea, please. I do not drink coffee.
A: Good. Is your family good?
B: Yes, thank you. My mother is happy. And you?
A: I feel hope and freedom. Goodbye, friend!
B: Goodbye!

22.title: The Nature
22.content: You know arbo (tree), mara (sea), rivo (river), monta (mountain), tera (earth), and sielo (sky). Today you go deeper into nature — flowers, fish, birds, and the ground beneath your feet.
22.aumaText: flora esi en arbo · pesa esi en mara · avi esi en sielo
22.englishText: a flower is on the tree · a fish is in the sea · a bird is in the sky

22.sentenceTranslation: the flower is red and beautiful
the seed is in the soil
the fish swims in the sea

22.title: Animals in nature
22.content: Combine nature words with movement verbs from Day 19 and colors from Day 16 to describe the living world.
22.aumaText: avi flugi en sielo · pesa nada en rivo · anima kuri en parko
22.englishText: the bird flies in the sky · the fish swims in the river · the animal runs in the park

22.sentenceTranslation: the bird flies in the blue sky
the animal is on the mountain
the wave is in the sea

22.question: How do you say 'the bird flies in the sky'?
22.explanation: Subject + verb + en + place: avi flugi en sielo.

22.sentenceTranslation: the rock is in the river

22.sentence: sema esi en sulo e sulo esi seka
22.sentenceTranslation: the seed is in the soil and the soil is dry

22.title: Nature descriptions
22.content: Use colors and weather to describe the natural world. Every pattern you know works here.
22.aumaText: flora esi yela · avi esi negra · petra esi en monta · onda esi en mara blu
22.englishText: the flower is yellow · the bird is black · the rock is on the mountain · the wave is in the blue sea

22.question: How do you say 'the fish swims in the river'?
22.explanation: Subject + verb + en + place: pesa nada en rivo.

22.question: How do you say 'the flower is red and the tree is green'?
options: flora esi roja e arbo esi verda
flora roja esi e arbo verda esi
22.explanation: Two descriptions joined with e: flora esi roja e arbo esi verda.

22.question: How do you say 'the seed is in the dry soil'?
22.explanation: sema esi en sulo seka — the descriptor seka follows the noun sulo.

23.title: The Direction
23.content: You know en (in/at), a (to), ante (before), and pos (after). Today you learn the four directions, plus spatial words that describe where things are in relation to each other.
23.aumaText: nori · sudi · esti · westi · supra · sub
23.englishText: north · south · east · west · above · under

23.sentenceTranslation: the mountain is in the north
the sea is in the south
the temple is in the east
the market is in the west

23.title: Above, under, between, and with
23.content: These spatial words describe relationships between things. They work as prepositions, just like en and a.
23.aumaText: luna esi supra monta · pesa esi sub onda · tomo esi inter arbo
23.englishText: the moon is above the mountain · the fish is under the wave · the house is between trees

23.sentenceTranslation: the fish is under the wave
the moon is above the mountain

23.question: How do you say 'the moon is above the mountain'?
23.explanation: Subject + esi + preposition + noun: luna esi supra monta.

23.sentence: parko esi inter tomo e merkato
23.sentenceTranslation: the park is between home and the market

23.sentence: mi anda kon ami di mi

23.title: Building spatial sentences
23.content: Combine directions and spatial words with everything you know — nature, places, family, and movement.
23.aumaText: mi anda kon mama di mi a parko · stela esi supra sielo · avi flugi supra arbo · rivo esi inter monta
23.englishText: I walk with my mother to the park · the star is above the sky · the bird flies above the tree · the river is between mountains

23.question: How do you say 'I walk with my friend'?
options: mi anda kon ami di mi
mi anda ami di mi kon
kon ami di mi mi anda
23.explanation: Subject + verb + kon + companion: mi anda kon ami di mi.

23.question: How do you say 'the fish is under the rock'?
23.explanation: Subject + esi + preposition + noun: pesa esi sub petra.

23.question: How do you say 'the temple is in the east'?
23.explanation: Subject + esi + en + direction: templo esi en esti.

24.title: The Craft
24.content: You know many action verbs already — anda, kuri, nada, koka, legi, skirvi. Today you learn verbs for building, creating, finding, and trying. These are the verbs of craft and work.
24.aumaText: mi konstrui tomo · mi pinta arbo · mi kresa flora
24.englishText: I build a house · I paint a tree · I grow a flower

24.sentence: mi konstrui tomo kon mano di mi
24.sentenceTranslation: I build a house with my hands

24.title: Making and fixing
24.content: Use these verbs with objects you already know — tomo (house), arbo (tree), flora (flower), pano (bread).
24.aumaText: mi repari tomo · mi konstrui kon petra · flora kresa en parko
24.englishText: I fix the house · I build with rocks · flowers grow in the park

24.sentence: mi repari tomo di ami di mi

24.sentenceTranslation: I find a rock in the river
I search for flowers in the park

24.question: How do you say 'I build a house with my hands'?
options: mi konstrui tomo kon mano di mi
mi konstrui kon mano di mi tomo
24.explanation: Subject + verb + object + kon + instrument: mi konstrui tomo kon mano di mi.

24.sentence: mi mostra flora a sina di mi
24.sentenceTranslation: I show the flower to my child

24.title: Combining craft verbs
24.content: Connect these verbs with porke, e, and pero to build complex sentences about what you do and why.
24.aumaText: mi serka e mi trova · mi proba konstrui tomo · mi pinta porke mi senti libre
24.englishText: I search and I find · I try to build a house · I paint because I feel free

24.question: How do you say 'I search for a flower in the park'?
24.explanation: Subject + verb + object + en + place: mi serka flora en parko.

24.question: How do you say 'flowers grow in good soil'?
24.explanation: Subject + verb + en + descriptor phrase: flora kresa en sulo bona.

24.question: How do you say 'I try and I find'?
24.explanation: Two actions joined with e: mi proba e mi trova.

25.title: The Spirit
25.content: You have learned the physical world — body, nature, food, movement. Today you learn words for the inner world: spirit, soul, courage, truth. These abstract words combine with senti and esi just like everything else.
25.aumaText: mi senti spirito · verao esi bona · mi senti kuraji
25.englishText: I feel spirit · truth is good · I feel courage

25.sentence: mi senti spirito en kora di mi
25.sentenceTranslation: I feel spirit in my heart

25.title: Freedom and courage
25.content: These words express deep human experiences. Use them with senti, esi, and voli.
25.aumaText: mi voli libra · mi senti kuraji · fida esi en kora
25.englishText: I want freedom · I feel courage · faith is in the heart

25.sentence: mi senti kuraji en kora di mi
25.sentenceTranslation: I feel courage in my heart

25.question: How do you say 'I feel spirit in my heart'?
options: mi senti spirito en kora di mi
mi spirito senti kora di mi
25.explanation: mi senti spirito (I feel spirit) + en kora di mi (in my heart).

25.title: The inner world
25.content: Combine spirit words with emotions and nature to express deep thoughts.
25.aumaText: spirito di mi esi libre · mi senti kuraji porke mi senti fida · verao esi en natura
25.englishText: my spirit is free · I feel courage because I feel faith · truth is in nature

25.question: How do you say 'truth is good'?
25.explanation: Subject + esi + descriptor: verao esi bona.

25.question: How do you say 'my soul is calm'?
25.explanation: animao di mi (my soul) + esi kalma (is calm).

25.question: How do you say 'I feel courage because I feel faith'?
options: mi senti kuraji porke mi senti fida
mi kuraji senti porke mi fida senti
porke mi senti fida mi senti kuraji
25.explanation: Feeling + porke + reason: mi senti kuraji porke mi senti fida.

26.title: The Speak
26.content: You know parla (speak), legi (read), and skirvi (write). Today you learn the things you speak, read, and write — words, phrases, books, and messages. These are the tools of communication.
26.aumaText: mi parla vorto · mi legi libro · mi skirvi mesaja
26.englishText: I speak words · I read a book · I write a message

26.sentenceTranslation: I write a sentence in Auma

26.title: Reading and writing
26.content: Combine book, letter, and message with legi (read) and skirvi (write).
26.aumaText: mi legi libro en sera · mi skirvi letera a ami · mi skirvi mesaja a mama
26.englishText: I read a book in the evening · I write a letter to a friend · I write a message to mother

26.sentenceTranslation: I read a book in the park

26.sentence: mi skirvi letera a papa di mi
26.sentenceTranslation: I write a letter to my father
I write a message to a friend

26.question: How do you say 'Auma is a good language'?
26.explanation: auma esi lingwa bona — subject + esi + noun + descriptor.

26.sentence: mi responda a mesaja di tu

26.title: Communication patterns
26.content: Use demanda and responda to describe conversation. These verbs follow the same pattern as all others.
26.aumaText: mi demanda e tu responda · mi legi libro e mi skirvi frasa · mi parla lingwa auma
26.englishText: I ask and you answer · I read a book and I write a sentence · I speak the Auma language

26.question: How do you say 'I read a book in the evening'?
26.explanation: Subject + verb + object + time: mi legi libro en sera.

26.question: How do you say 'I ask and you answer'?
26.explanation: Two actions joined with e: mi demanda e tu responda.

26.question: How do you say 'I write a letter to my friend'?
options: mi skirvi letera a ami di mi
mi letera skirvi ami di mi a
26.explanation: Subject + verb + object + a + recipient: mi skirvi letera a ami di mi.

27.title: The Society
27.content: You have words for the self, family, nature, and spirit. Today you learn about community — coming together, singing, dancing, and celebrating. These are the words that connect people.
27.aumaText: we kanta e we dansa · komunita esi bona · we senti unita
27.englishText: we sing and we dance · community is good · we feel unity

27.title: Music and dance
27.content: Singing and dancing bring community together. Use these verbs with places and with kon (with).
27.aumaText: we kanta en festa · we dansa kon ami · musika esi bona
27.englishText: we sing at the festival · we dance with friends · music is good

27.sentence: we kanta kon amili di we

27.question: How do you say 'we sing and we dance at the festival'?
options: we kanta e we dansa en festa
we kanta dansa en festa e
kanta we e dansa we festa en
27.explanation: Two actions joined with e + location: we kanta e we dansa en festa.

27.sentenceTranslation: the ritual is at the temple

27.sentence: we senti armoni en komunita di we
27.sentenceTranslation: we feel harmony in our community

27.title: Community and spirit
27.content: Combine community words with spirit words from Day 25 to express deep ideas about togetherness.
27.aumaText: komunita esi unita e armoni · we senti spirito en ritua · we kanta e we senti libre
27.englishText: community is unity and harmony · we feel spirit in the ritual · we sing and we feel free

27.question: How do you say 'we feel harmony in our community'?
options: we senti armoni en komunita di we
we armoni senti komunita di we en
27.explanation: Subject + verb + object + en + possessive phrase: we senti armoni en komunita di we.

27.question: How do you say 'the ritual is at the temple'?
27.explanation: Subject + esi + en + place: ritua esi en templo.

27.question: How do you say 'music is good and we dance'?
options: musika esi bona e we dansa
musika bona esi e dansa we
bona musika e we dansa esi
27.explanation: Two statements joined with e: musika esi bona e we dansa.

28.title: The Celebration
28.content: You have completed four weeks of Auma. You know over one hundred words. You can talk about yourself, your family, your feelings, the natural world, directions, crafts, spirit, language, and community. This is your graduation.

28.title: Everything you know
28.content: Week 1: core structure — pronouns, numbers, possessives, emotions, requests, days, greetings. Week 2: navigation — places, time, daily verbs, connectors, nature, body. Week 3: depth — food, colors, family, weather, movement, deeper feelings. Week 4: expression — nature details, directions, craft verbs, spirit, language, community.
28.aumaText: mi parla auma · mi skirvi frasa · mi legi libro · mi kanta e mi dansa · mi senti libre e mi senti spirito
28.englishText: I speak Auma · I write sentences · I read books · I sing and I dance · I feel free and I feel spirit

28.question: How do you say 'the bird flies above the tree'?
28.explanation: Subject + verb + preposition + noun: avi flugi supra arbo.

28.question: How do you say 'I build a house with rocks'?
28.explanation: Subject + verb + object + kon + material: mi konstrui tomo kon petra.

28.question: How do you say 'we feel unity in our community'?
options: we senti unita en komunita di we
senti we unita komunita di we en
28.explanation: we senti unita (we feel unity) + en komunita di we (in our community).

28.question: How do you say 'truth is in nature'?
28.explanation: Subject + esi + en + noun: verao esi en natura.

28.question: How do you say 'I search and I find'?
28.explanation: Two actions joined with e: mi serka e mi trova.

28.question: How do you say 'Auma is a good language and I feel happy'?
options: auma esi lingwa bona e mi senti hapi
auma lingwa bona esi e hapi mi senti
lingwa auma bona esi e senti mi hapi
28.explanation: Two statements joined with e: auma esi lingwa bona e mi senti hapi.

28.question: How do you ask 'do you feel courage?'
28.explanation: ka at the start: ka tu senti kuraji?

28.title: Extended reading
28.content: Read this passage aloud. Every word is one you have learned across all four weeks.
28.aumaText: salama, ami! hodi esi solosa e kalda. sielo esi blu e flora esi roja en parko. mi anda kon mama di mi a merkato. we kani fruta e we beva suko. pos, mi kuri en via e mi senti libre. avi flugi supra arbo verda. pesa nada en rivo blu. mi pensa e mi senti spirito en kora di mi. sera esi kalma. we kanta e we dansa en tomo. amili di mi esi bona. mi senti amora e kontenta. mi skirvi frasa en auma porke mi ama lingwa di we. verao esi en natura. we senti unita e armoni. gratao. adio!
28.englishText: Hello, friend! Today is sunny and warm. The sky is blue and flowers are red in the park. I walk with my mother to the market. We eat fruit and we drink juice. After, I run on the road and I feel free. A bird flies above a green tree. Fish swim in the blue river. I think and I feel spirit in my heart. The evening is calm. We sing and we dance at home. My family is good. I feel love and contentment. I write sentences in Auma because I love our language. Truth is in nature. We feel unity and harmony. Thank you. Goodbye!

28.title: Full conversation
28.content: Practice this conversation. It uses vocabulary from all four weeks.
28.aumaText:
A: salama! ka tu esi bona hodi?
B: ya, mi esi kontenta. klima esi solosa!
A: mi kuri en parko en matina. mi trova flora roja e yela.
B: bona! mi pinta en sera. mi pinta arbo e monta.
A: ka amili di tu esi bona?
B: ya. mama di mi koka sopa e papa di mi legi en sera.
A: we voli festa! we kanta e dansa kon komunita di we.
B: ya! mi senti armoni e spirito. auma esi lingwa bona.
A: verao! mi senti amora e spera. adio, ami.
B: adio! mi senti gratao.
28.englishText:
A: Hello! Are you good today?
B: Yes, I am content. The weather is sunny!
A: I run in the park in the morning. I find red and yellow flowers.
B: Good! I paint in the evening. I paint trees and mountains.
A: Is your family good?
B: Yes. My mother cooks soup and my father reads in the evening.
A: We want a festival! We sing and dance with our community.
B: Yes! I feel harmony and spirit. Auma is a good language.
A: Truth! I feel love and hope. Goodbye, friend.
B: Goodbye! I feel grateful.

28.title: Graduation
28.content: You have completed the Auma Foundation. Twenty-eight days. Over one hundred words. Every sentence follows the same shape: Subject, Verb, Object. No exceptions, no irregulars, no hidden rules. You built this knowledge one day at a time — from mi esi bona to conversations about spirit, community, and truth. The foundation is complete. The language of light is in you.
28.aumaText: gratao, ami. tu esi pronta. spirito di tu esi libre. kora di tu esi bona. we senti unita e armoni. salama.
28.englishText: Thank you, friend. You are ready. Your spirit is free. Your heart is good. We feel unity and harmony. Peace.

29.title: The Question
29.content: In Foundation you learned to ask yes/no questions with ka. Now you unlock the seven question words that let you ask open-ended questions — who, what, where, when, why, how, and how much. Plus a conjunction for when.
29.aumaText: ka tu esi bona? → sapa esi tu?
29.englishText: are you good? → who are you?

29.title: Question words replace ka
29.content: When you use a question word, you do not need ka. The question word itself marks the sentence as a question. It goes at the beginning, just like ka did.
29.aumaText: ka tu esi bona? → komo tu esi?
29.englishText: are you good? → how are you?

29.question: How do you ask 'where is the market?'
29.explanation: Question word at the start: ketu esi merkato?

29.sentence: mi esi hapi kwando mi kanta
29.sentenceTranslation: I am happy when I sing

29.title: kemo vs. kwando
29.content: kemo asks a question: kemo tu beni? (when do you come?). kwando connects two ideas: mi esi hapi kwando mi kanta (I am happy when I sing). One asks, the other joins.
29.aumaText: kemo tu beni? · mi dansa kwando mi esi hapi
29.englishText: when do you come? · I dance when I am happy

29.title: Building real conversations
29.content: With these seven question words plus kwando, you can now ask anything. Combine them with all your Foundation vocabulary for full conversational power.
29.aumaText: sapa koka sopa? · kosa esi en merkato? · ketu amili di tu esi? · kemo tu legi libro?
29.englishText: who cooks soup? · what is at the market? · where is your family? · when do you read a book?

29.question: How do you ask 'why do you feel sad?'
29.explanation: Question word first: keno tu senti trisa?

29.question: How do you say 'I cook when the morning is sunny'?
options: mi koka kwando matina esi solosa
mi koka kemo matina esi solosa
kwando mi koka matina esi solosa
29.explanation: kwando joins two clauses: mi koka kwando matina esi solosa.

29.question: How do you ask 'how many fish are in the river?'
29.explanation: kwanto asks about quantity: kwanto pesa esi en rivo?

30.title: The Ability
30.content: Foundation gave you action verbs — anda, koka, legi. Now you learn modal verbs that modify those actions. These go before the main verb: mi pude anda (I can walk). The main verb stays unchanged.

30.sentenceTranslation: I can swim in the sea

30.title: Modal + verb pattern
30.content: The modal always comes directly before the main verb. Subject + modal + verb + object. Negation goes before the modal: mi no pude anda.
30.aumaText: mi pude koka · mi debe lava · mi no devri kuri
30.englishText: I can cook · I must wash · I should not run

30.question: How do you say 'you should not eat at night'?
options: tu no devri kani en noka
tu devri no kani en noka
no tu devri kani en noka
30.explanation: Negation before the modal: tu no devri kani en noka.

30.sentenceTranslation: the spirit soars in the sky

30.title: Combining modals with questions
30.content: Use question words with modals naturally. The question word comes first, then the normal modal + verb pattern.
30.aumaText: kosa tu pude koka? · kemo we debe anda? · keno tu no pude domi?
30.englishText: what can you cook? · when must we walk? · why can you not sleep?

30.question: How do you ask 'what can you hear?'
30.explanation: Question word + subject + modal + verb: kosa tu pude rongo?

30.question: How do you say 'we need to cook soup'?
30.explanation: Subject + modal + verb + object: we neda koka sopa.

30.question: How do you say 'the bird might soar above the mountain'?
30.explanation: Subject + modal + verb + preposition + noun: spirito moga lani supra monta.

31.title: The Degree
31.content: You can describe things as bona, kalda, or granda. Now you learn to compare them — more, less, most, very. These modifier words go directly before the quality they modify.

31.sentenceTranslation: the flower is the least big

31.title: Comparison pattern
31.content: Place the modifier directly before the quality. plu bona = more good, maks bona = most good, min kalda = less hot. The pattern never changes.
31.aumaText: plu kalda · maks frida · min lasa · minim trisa
31.englishText: hotter · coldest · less tired · least sad

31.question: How do you say 'the morning is colder'?
31.explanation: plu before the quality: matina esi plu frida.

31.title: Intensifiers in conversation
31.content: mui (very), tro (too much), basta (enough), and kasi (almost) add precision to everything you say. Combine them with questions and modals for natural speech.
31.aumaText: keno tu esi tro lasa? · mi esi mui hapi kwando mi kanta · mi kasi pude nada
31.englishText: why are you too tired? · I am very happy when I sing · I can almost swim

31.question: How do you say 'the soup is very good'?
31.explanation: mui for very: sopa esi mui bona.

31.question: How do you say 'I am almost calm'?
31.explanation: kasi before the quality: mi esi kasi kalma.

31.question: How do you say 'the river is the best'?
31.explanation: maks for superlative: rivo esi maks bona.

31.sentenceTranslation: many friends come to the celebration

32.title: The Calendar
32.content: Foundation gave you dina (day), matina (morning), sera (evening). Now you expand into larger and smaller time units — weeks, months, years, hours, minutes, seconds, and seasons.

32.title: Smaller time units
32.content: For precise time, Auma uses hori (hour), minuta (minute), and sekunda (second). These follow the same patterns as all other nouns.
32.aumaText: un hori teni sisa-des minuta · un minuta teni sisa-des sekunda
32.englishText: one hour has sixty minutes · one minute has sixty seconds

32.question: How do you say 'I run for one hour every morning'?
options: mi kuri un hori en matina
mi kuri hori un en matina
mi en matina kuri un hori
32.explanation: mi kuri (I run) + un hori (one hour) + en matina (in the morning).

32.sentenceTranslation: how many seasons are in a year?

32.title: Time in conversation
32.content: Combine these new time words with your question words and modals for natural conversation about schedules and plans.
32.aumaText: kemo tu pude beni? · mi debe anda en du hori · kosa tu koka iti semana?
32.englishText: when can you come? · I must walk in two hours · what do you cook this week?

32.question: How do you say 'a year has twelve months'?
32.explanation: anua teni (a year has) + des-du mensa (twelve months).

32.question: How do you ask 'how many hours do you need?'
32.explanation: kwanto + noun + subject + verb: kwanto hori tu neda?

33.title: The Flow
33.content: Real conversation needs filler and flow words — the small signals that show you are thinking, agreeing, emphasizing, or shifting topic. These discourse markers make your Auma sound natural.
33.aumaText: beno, mi pensa ke... · klaro! · enton, kosa we pude?
33.englishText: well, I think that... · clearly! · so, what do we do?

33.sentence: beno, mi pensa ke tu esi bona
33.sentenceTranslation: well, I think that you are good

33.question: How do you say 'well, I think the mountain is very big'?
options: beno, mi pensa ke monta esi mui granda
beno, monta esi mui granda mi pensa
mi pensa beno ke monta esi mui granda
33.explanation: beno at the start, then a normal sentence with ke: beno, mi pensa ke monta esi mui granda.

33.sentenceTranslation: look, a bird flies above the tree!
alright, I walk to the market

33.title: Natural speech patterns
33.content: Discourse markers typically go at the start of a sentence or clause. They do not change the grammar — they add attitude and flow.
33.aumaText: klaro, tu pude beni! · serio, mi neda akwa · aktua, mi no pude
33.englishText: of course, you can come! · seriously, I need water · actually, I cannot

33.question: How do you say 'look, the weather is very good today!'?
options: mira, klima esi mui bona hodi!
klima mira esi mui bona hodi!
mira klima hodi esi mui bona!
33.explanation: mira at the start: mira, klima esi mui bona hodi!

33.question: How do you say 'alright, so what do you want?'
33.explanation: bweno and enton stack naturally: bweno, enton kosa tu voli?

34.title: The Mind
34.content: Foundation gave you pensa (think) and senti (feel). Now you gain verbs for the full range of mental activity — deciding, believing, proving, knowing, doubting, paying, dreaming, and standing firm.
34.aumaText: mi pensa → mi deside · mi senti → mi kreda
34.englishText: I think → I decide · I feel → I believe

34.sentence: mi deside a anda en matina
34.sentenceTranslation: I decide to walk in the morning

34.sentence: mi kreda ke tu esi bona
34.sentenceTranslation: I believe that you are good

34.sentence: tu prova ke tu esi forta
34.sentenceTranslation: you prove that you are strong

34.title: Using ke (that) with mental verbs
34.content: You learned ke in Foundation as a conjunction. With mental verbs, ke introduces what you think, believe, know, or doubt. The pattern is: subject + verb + ke + clause.
34.aumaText: mi kreda ke natura esi bona · mi sabe ke tu pude koka
34.englishText: I believe that nature is good · I know that you can cook

34.question: How do you say 'I decide that I must run'?
options: mi deside ke mi debe kuri
deside mi ke mi debe kuri
34.explanation: mi deside ke (I decide that) + mi debe kuri (I must run).

34.sentence: mi no duba ke tu esi ami
34.sentenceTranslation: I do not doubt that you are a friend

34.title: Building complex thoughts
34.content: With these verbs you can express belief, decision, doubt, knowledge, and dreams. Combined with modals and question words, your sentences become rich and natural.
34.aumaText: mi kreda ke we pude trova armoni · keno tu duba? · mi ruma ke mi flugi supra monta
34.englishText: I believe that we can find harmony · why do you doubt? · I dream that I soar above the mountain

34.question: How do you say 'I know that the river is cold'?
options: mi sabe ke rivo esi frida
sabe mi ke rivo esi frida
34.explanation: mi sabe ke (I know that) + rivo esi frida (the river is cold).

34.question: How do you say 'they dream at night and stand in the morning'?
options: tali ruma en noka e standi en matina
tali ruma e standi en noka en matina
en noka tali ruma e en matina standi
34.explanation: Two actions joined with e: tali ruma en noka e standi en matina.

35.title: Week 5 Checkpoint
35.content: You have unlocked conversational mastery tools: question words (sapa, kosa, ketu, kemo, keno, komo, kwanto), modals (pude, debe, devri, moga, neda), comparisons (plu, maks, min, mui, tro, basta, kasi), time depth (semana, mensa, anua, tempo, hori), discourse markers (klaro, beno, enton, serio), and mental verbs (deside, kreda, sabe, duba). Now practice combining them all.

35.title: Conversation practice
35.content: Read this exchange aloud. Every word is from Foundation or Week 5.
35.aumaText:
A: salama! komo tu senti hodi?
B: beno, mi senti tipo lasa. mi no domi bona en noka.
A: serio? keno tu no pude domi?
B: mi pensa tro. mi debe ruma plu kalma.
A: klaro. tu devri beva teo en sera. teo esi mui kalma.
B: bweno, mi deside ke mi beva teo hodi sera. gratao, ami.
A: klaro! mi sabe ke tu pude senti plu bona doma.
B: mi kreda ke tu esi bona. adio!
35.englishText:
A: Hello! How do you feel today?
B: Well, I feel sort of tired. I did not sleep well at night.
A: Seriously? Why could you not sleep?
B: I think too much. I must dream more calmly.
A: Of course. You should drink tea in the evening. Tea is very calming.
B: Alright, I decide that I will drink tea this evening. Thank you, friend.
A: Of course! I know that you can feel better tomorrow.
B: I believe that you are right. Goodbye!

35.question: How do you ask 'who can sing the most?'
35.explanation: sapa (who) + pude kanta (can sing) + maks bona (the best).

35.question: How do you say 'I know that we must pay at the market'?
options: mi sabe ke we debe lipa en merkato
mi sabe we debe lipa en merkato
sabe mi ke we lipa debe en merkato
35.explanation: mi sabe ke (I know that) + we debe lipa (we must pay) + en merkato (at the market).

35.question: How do you say 'actually, the year has four seasons'?
35.explanation: aktua at the start: aktua, anua teni fo sezoni.

35.question: Build this sentence: 'I almost decided, but I doubt'
options: mi kasi deside, pero mi duba
mi deside kasi, pero mi duba
kasi mi deside, pero duba mi
35.explanation: mi kasi deside (I almost decided) + pero (but) + mi duba (I doubt).

36.title: The Abstract
36.content: Foundation gave you spirito, natura, libra, verao. Now you go deeper into the vocabulary of thought — idea, purpose, destiny, force, meaning, consciousness, thought, and memory. In v12, kira is consciousness/living knowledge; konosia is factual or stored knowledge; sabe is the verb to know.
36.aumaText: mi pensa → mi teni idea · mi sabe → mi teni konosia · mi senti kira
36.englishText: I think → I have an idea · I know → I have knowledge · I feel consciousness

36.question: How do you say 'my purpose is to find truth'?
options: purpusa di mi esi trova verao
trova verao esi purpusa di mi
36.explanation: purpusa di mi (my purpose) + esi (is) + trova verao (to find truth).
