36.sentence: kosa esi signifa di iti vorto?
36.sentenceTranslation: what is the meaning of this word?

36.sentence: penso di mi esi klaro hodi

36.title: Abstract nouns in sentences
36.content: Abstract nouns work exactly like concrete nouns. They follow the same subject + verb + object pattern and can be possessed with di.
36.aumaText: idea di tu esi mui bona · mi sabe ke kira esi en mi · signifa di ama esi granda
36.englishText: your idea is very good · I know that consciousness is in me · the meaning of love is great

36.question: How do you ask 'what is the meaning of this?'
36.explanation: kosa esi (what is) + signifa di iti (the meaning of this).

36.question: How do you say 'memory is stronger than thought'?
options: memo esi plu forta ke penso
penso esi min forta ke memo
36.explanation: memo esi plu forta ke penso (memory is stronger than thought).

37.title: The Bond
37.content: Foundation taught you festa, dansa, musika, kanta for celebration. Now you learn the intimate social words — joking, laughing, hugging, kissing, promising, secrets, trust, and face/dignity.
37.aumaText: we dansa → we joka e riso
37.englishText: we dance → we joke and laugh

37.sentence: mi joka kon ami di mi

37.sentenceTranslation: I give a hug to my mother

37.question: How do you say 'we laugh and hug at the festival'?
options: we riso e dona abraso en festa
we riso abraso en festa e
riso we e abraso en festa dona
37.explanation: we riso e dona abraso en festa.

37.sentenceTranslation: I give a promise to you

37.sentence: mianzi esi mui importa en komunita
37.sentenceTranslation: face is very important in community

37.title: Social bonds in Auma
37.content: These words describe the gestures and trust that hold communities together. Notice how they combine naturally with Foundation words like dona (give), ami (friend), and komunita (community).
37.aumaText: mi konfida en ami di mi · we dona promisa a komunita di we · mi teni sekreta pero mi devri parla
37.englishText: I trust in my friend · we give a promise to our community · I have a secret but I should speak

37.question: How do you say 'I promise that I will not reveal the secret'?
options: mi dona promisa ke mi no parla sekreta
mi promisa no parla sekreta dona
dona mi promisa ke sekreta mi no parla
37.explanation: mi dona promisa ke (I promise that) + mi no parla sekreta (I will not speak the secret).

37.question: How do you ask 'who do you trust?'
37.explanation: sapa (who) + tu konfida en (do you trust in).

38.title: The Digital
38.content: The modern world needs modern words. Auma has a full technology vocabulary that follows the same transparent patterns. No irregular forms — just new nouns and verbs for the digital world.

38.sentenceTranslation: the network is in the house

38.question: How do you say 'I read a book on the computer'?
38.explanation: mi legi libro (I read a book) + en komputa (on the computer).

38.sentenceTranslation: I send a message to a friend
the message from a friend is good
I receive a message from a friend

38.title: Digital communication
38.content: senda (send) and resiva (receive) pair naturally with mesaja (message), letera (letter), and the prepositions a (to) and de (from).
38.aumaText: mi senda mesaja a amili di mi · mi resiva letera de mama · mi tekla en telefona
38.englishText: I send a message to my family · I receive a letter from my mother · I type on the phone

38.question: How do you ask 'when do you send the message?'
38.explanation: kemo (when, question) + tu senda mesaja (do you send the message).

38.question: How do you say 'the phone screen is too small'?
options: skirmo di telefona esi tro pika
skirmo telefona di esi pika tro
38.explanation: skirmo di telefona (the phone screen) + esi tro pika (is too small).

39.title: The Exchange
39.content: Business and commerce use the same sentence patterns as everything else in Auma. Learn eight essential words for prices, contracts, and transactions.

39.sentenceTranslation: I reserve a building in the city

39.question: How do you ask 'what is the price of coffee?'

39.sentence: mi anda a ofisa en matina
39.sentenceTranslation: I go to the office in the morning
I have an account on the internet

39.title: Business conversation
39.content: Combine these with modals and question words for natural business exchanges at the merkato or ofisa.
39.aumaText: mi voli rezerva, plisa · kosa esi preka? · mi pude kansela? · mi debe lipa nau
39.englishText: I want to reserve, please · what is the price? · can I cancel? · I must pay now

39.question: How do you say 'I must cancel the reservation'?
How do you say 'the office is in the city'?
39.explanation: ofisa esi en sita (the office is in the city).

40.title: The Season
40.content: You learned sezoni (season) on Day 32. Now you name the seasons themselves — summer, winter, spring, autumn, dry season, and rainy season. Plus two time-quality words.

40.sentenceTranslation: the tree is golden in autumn

40.question: How do you ask 'which season do you like most?'
40.explanation: kosa sezoni (which season) + tu ama maks (do you love most).

40.sentenceTranslation: dry season is hot and dry

40.title: Seasons in conversation
40.content: Seasons are nouns that work like any other. Use them with prepositions, comparisons, and question words.
40.aumaText: mi ama verino plu ke hiverno · estino esi maks kalda · en otunio mi senti kalma · pluvino dona akwa
40.englishText: I love spring more than winter · summer is the hottest · in autumn I feel calm · rainy season gives water

40.question: How do you say 'winter is colder than autumn'?
options: hiverno esi plu frida ke otunio
otunio esi min frida ke hiverno
40.explanation: hiverno esi plu frida ke otunio (winter is colder than autumn).

40.question: How do you say 'time moves slowly in winter'?
options: tempo esi lenta mota en hiverno
40.explanation: tempo mota lenta (time moves slowly) + en hiverno (in winter).

41.title: The Depth
41.content: Foundation gave you basic emotions — hapi, trisa, ira, kalma. Now you explore the nuanced emotional landscape: inspired, courageous-hearted, creative, cozy, focused, distracted, tense, and patient.

41.sentence: mi senti inspira kwando mi legi
41.sentenceTranslation: I feel inspired when I read
the house is cozy in winter

41.question: How do you say 'I feel inspired and creative'?

41.sentence: mi esi foka kwando mi skirvi
41.sentenceTranslation: I am focused when I write
I am distracted in the office

41.title: Emotional nuance
41.content: These words let you describe subtle inner states. Combine them with modals, comparisons, and discourse markers for authentic expression.
41.aumaText: mi senti plu inspira en matina · mi esi tro tensa, mi neda esi plu kalma · aktua, mi senti huga en tomo
41.englishText: I feel more inspired in the morning · I am too tense, I need to be calmer · actually, I feel cozy at home

41.question: How do you say 'you should be more focused and less distracted'?
options: tu devri esi plu foka e min distra
tu devri plu foka e min distra esi
plu foka tu devri esi e min distra
41.explanation: tu devri esi (you should be) + plu foka e min distra (more focused and less distracted).

41.question: How do you say 'patience is very important'?
41.explanation: sabar esi mui importa (patience is very important).

42.title: Week 6 Checkpoint
42.content: This week you explored culture and expression: abstract concepts (idea, purpusa, destina, signifa, kira, penso, memo), social bonds (joka, riso, abraso, besa, promisa, sekreta, konfida), technology (komputa, telefona, reta, skirmo, tekla, klika, senda, resiva), business (preka, rezerva, kontrato, diskonto, kansela, ofisa, konto, karga), seasons (verino, estino, otunio, hiverno, sekino, pluvino), and nuanced emotions (inspira, kori, kreati, huga, foka, distra, tensa, sabar).

42.title: Extended reading
42.content: Read this passage aloud. It combines Foundation and Expansion vocabulary.
42.aumaText: salama, ami! komo tu senti hodi? mi esi inspira porke verino esi inspira. mi senda mesaja a amili di mi en telefona. mi skirvi: salama! mira, flora kresa en parko! mi teni idea nova — we devri dona festa en komunita di we kwando estino beni. we pude koka sopa e kani fruta. mi debe lipa preka en merkato pero mi kreda ke diskonto esi bona. mi konfida en tu. tu esi kori e kreati. enton, kosa tu pensa? mi senti huga kwando we esi en tomo kon amili di we. adio!
42.englishText: Hello, friend! How do you feel today? I am inspired because spring is near. I send a message to my family on the phone. I write: Hello! Look, flowers grow in the park! I have a new idea — we should have a festival in our community when summer comes. We can cook soup and eat fruit. I must pay the price at the market but I believe that the discount is good. I trust in you. You are courageous and creative. So, what do you think? I feel cozy when we are at home with our family. Goodbye!

42.question: How do you say 'I send a promise to my community'?
options: mi senda promisa a komunita di mi
mi dona promisa senda a komunita
senda mi promisa a komunita di mi
42.explanation: mi senda promisa (I send a promise) + a komunita di mi (to my community).

42.question: How do you say 'consciousness is the force of thought'?
42.explanation: kira esi (consciousness is) + forsa di penso (the force of thought).

42.question: How do you ask 'can I cancel the reservation?'
42.explanation: ka + mi pude kansela rezerva: ka mi pude kansela rezerva?

42.question: How do you say 'I joke and laugh when I am with friends'?
options: mi joka e riso kwando mi esi kon amili
mi joka riso kwando mi esi amili kon
kwando mi esi kon amili mi joka riso e
42.explanation: mi joka e riso (I joke and laugh) + kwando mi esi kon amili (when I am with friends).

43.title: The Healing
43.content: Foundation taught you korpo (body), kora (heart), dolo (pain), sana (heal). Now you expand into the full health vocabulary — medicine, wellness, balance, rest, energy, strength, cure, and life.

43.sentence: tu debe reposa kwando tu esi lasa
43.sentenceTranslation: you must rest when you are tired

43.question: How do you say 'health is more important than money'?
43.explanation: sanita esi plu importa (health is more important).

43.sentence: mi teni molta eneria en matina
43.sentenceTranslation: I have much energy in the morning

43.title: Health philosophy in Auma
43.content: Auma health vocabulary connects body, mind, and spirit. Balance, rest, energy, and strength are all part of the same conversation.
43.aumaText: mi neda balansa inter eneria e reposa · kurita beni de natura e kalma · fortia di korpo e fortia di penso esi mui importa
43.englishText: I need balance between energy and rest · healing comes from nature and calm · strength of body and strength of mind are very important

43.question: How do you say 'I need to rest because I have no energy'?
options: mi neda reposa porke mi no teni eneria
mi reposa neda porke mi eneria no teni
porke mi no teni eneria mi neda reposa
43.explanation: mi neda reposa (I need to rest) + porke (because) + mi no teni eneria (I have no energy).

43.question: How do you say 'life has balance between strength and rest'?
options: vita teni balansa inter fortia e reposa
vita esi balansa fortia e reposa inter
balansa di vita teni fortia e reposa
43.explanation: vita teni balansa (life has balance) + inter fortia e reposa (between strength and rest).

44.title: The Wild
44.content: Foundation gave you monta, mara, arbo, flora. Now you explore the wilder side of nature — coral reefs, islands, forests, butterflies, palm trees, lotus flowers, bamboo, and coconut.

44.sentenceTranslation: the island is in the blue sea
the forest is green and big
the butterfly flies in the forest

44.question: How do you say 'the island has a beautiful forest'?
44.explanation: isla teni boski bona (the island has a beautiful forest).

44.sentenceTranslation: palm trees grow on the island
the lotus is the flower of calm

44.title: Describing the wild world
44.content: These nature words combine beautifully with comparisons, prepositions, and your full vocabulary for rich descriptions of the natural world.
44.aumaText: korali esi plu bona ke petra en mara · maripi esi maks bona en boski · mi senti inspira kwando mi esi en isla
44.englishText: coral is more beautiful than rock in the sea · the butterfly is the most beautiful in the forest · I feel inspired when I am on the island

44.question: How do you say 'bamboo is the fastest-growing plant'?
44.explanation: bambu kresa maks rapida (bamboo grows the fastest).

44.question: How do you say 'I find lotus flowers at the river'?
44.explanation: mi trova loto (I find lotus) + en rivo (at the river).

45.title: The Position
45.content: Foundation gave you en, a, sub, supra, inter, kon. Now you add advanced prepositions that express opposition, distance, surrounding, passing through, nearness, absence, purpose, and movement under.

45.sentence: isla esi lonta de tomo di mi
45.sentenceTranslation: the island is far from my house
the river moves through the forest

45.question: How do you say 'the bird flies through the forest'?
45.explanation: avi flugi (the bird flies) + tra boski (through the forest).

45.sentenceTranslation: the cafe is near the park

45.sentence: mi no pude domi sen kalma
mi koka por amili di mi

45.sentenceTranslation: the fish swims under the wave

45.title: Prepositions in rich sentences
45.content: Stack prepositions with your full vocabulary to describe complex spatial relationships and purposes.
45.aumaText: mi anda tra boski e sirka parko por trova kalma · tomo di mi esi proksima mara pero lonta de merkato · we standi kontra pluva sen dolo
45.englishText: I walk through the forest and around the park to find calm · my house is near the sea but far from the market · we stand against the rain without pain

45.question: How do you say 'I cook for my friend without bread'?
options: mi koka por ami di mi sen pano
mi koka sen pano por ami di mi
por ami di mi mi koka sen pano
45.explanation: mi koka (I cook) + por ami di mi (for my friend) + sen pano (without bread).

45.question: How do you say 'the temple is near the mountain'?
45.explanation: templo esi proksima monta (the temple is near the mountain).

46.title: The Feast
46.content: Foundation gave you akwa, pano, kopi, teo, sopa, fruta, karni, ovo. Now you deepen the food vocabulary with words for food itself, honey, flavor, spice, sugar, salt, cheese, and hunger.

46.sentence: kibo esi mui importa por vita
46.sentenceTranslation: food is very important for life

46.sentence: sabora di sopa esi mui bona
46.sentenceTranslation: the flavor of the soup is very good

46.question: How do you say 'the honey has a good flavor'?
46.explanation: miela teni sabora bona (the honey has a good flavor).

46.title: Describing food and meals
46.content: Combine food words with flavors, comparisons, and feelings for rich descriptions of meals and eating.
46.aumaText: mi senti hambre en sera · sabora di keso esi plu forta ke sabora di pano · mi koka kon salo, spisao, e miela
46.englishText: I feel hungry in the evening · the flavor of cheese is stronger than the flavor of bread · I cook with salt, spice, and honey

46.question: How do you say 'I am too hungry, I need food'?
options: mi esi tro hambre, mi neda kibo
mi tro hambre esi, mi kibo neda
tro hambre mi esi, neda mi kibo
46.explanation: mi esi tro hambre (I am too hungry) + mi neda kibo (I need food).

46.question: How do you ask 'does the soup need more salt?'
46.explanation: ka + sopa neda plu salo: ka sopa neda plu salo?

47.title: The Absence
47.content: Foundation gave you no (not). Auma has a complete negation system built from question words: nokemo (never), nokeno (for no reason), nomodo (in no way), nosapa (no one). Plus words for emptiness and falseness.
47.englishText: not → never, for no reason, in no way, no one

47.sentence: mi nokemo senti sola en komunita
47.sentenceTranslation: I never feel alone in community
I am not angry for no reason
this is in no way good

47.title: The negation pattern
47.content: Each negation word mirrors a question word: kemo (when?) becomes nokemo (never), keno (why?) becomes nokeno (for no reason), komo (how?) becomes nomodo (in no way), sapa (who?) becomes nosapa (no one). This is systematic — no memorization needed.
47.aumaText: kemo → nokemo · keno → nokeno · komo → nomodo · sapa → nosapa
47.englishText: when? → never · why? → for no reason · how? → in no way · who? → no one

47.question: How do you say 'no one can fly'?
47.explanation: nosapa pude flugi (no one can fly). nosapa already contains the negation.

47.sentence: malu no esi forta ke bona
47.sentenceTranslation: bad is not stronger than good

47.title: Negation in conversation
47.content: These words let you express absence, emptiness, and falseness with precision. They make your Auma more nuanced and natural.
47.aumaText: mi nokemo parla falsa vorto · nosapa pude esi en du tomo en un tempo · tomo vakua dona kalma
47.englishText: I never speak false words · no one can be in two places at the same time · an empty room gives calm

47.question: How do you say 'I never eat without salt'?
options: mi no kani nokemo sen salo
47.explanation: mi nokemo kani sen salo (I never eat without salt).

47.question: How do you say 'the house is empty, there is nothing'?
options: tomo esi vakua, nulao esi en ita
tomo esi vakua, esi nulao en ita
47.explanation: tomo esi vakua (the house is empty) + nulao esi en ita (nothing is in it).

48.title: The Voice
48.content: Foundation gave you vorto, frasa, lingwa, libro, mesaja. Now you deepen into opinion, poetry, signs, art, story, fact, song (noun), and the English language word.

48.sentence: mi skirvi poema por ami di mi
48.sentenceTranslation: I write a poem for my friend
the sign is on the road
art is the language of the heart

48.question: How do you say 'I believe that art is very important'?
options: mi kreda ke arto esi mui importa
mi kreda arto esi mui importa
mi kreda ke mui importa esi arto
48.explanation: mi kreda ke (I believe that) + arto esi mui importa (art is very important).

48.sentenceTranslation: the elder speaks the story of the community
this is a fact, not an opinion

48.title: Communication and culture
48.content: With these words you can discuss opinion versus fact, appreciate art and poetry, tell stories, and talk about languages.
48.aumaText: opina di mi esi ke poema esi maks bona via di arto · storia di tana teni konosia · fakta esi bona, opina pude esi falsa
48.englishText: my opinion is that poetry is the most beautiful form of art · the elder's story has wisdom · facts are good, opinions can be false

48.question: How do you say 'I write songs and poems in Auma'?
options: mi skirvi kanto e poema en auma
mi skirvi en auma kanto e poema
en auma mi skirvi kanto poema e
48.explanation: mi skirvi kanto e poema en auma.

48.question: How do you ask 'do you speak English or Auma?'
options: ka tu parla angla o auma?
tu parla angla o auma ka?
ka parla tu angla o auma?
48.explanation: ka tu parla angla o auma?

49.title: Week 7 Checkpoint
49.content: This week took you deeper: health and healing (medika, sanita, balansa, reposa, eneria, fortia, kurita, vita), wild nature (korali, isla, boski, maripi, palma, loto, bambu, koko), advanced prepositions (kontra, lonta, sirka, tra, proksima, sen, por, suba), rich food vocabulary (kibo, miela, sabora, spisao, suka, salo, keso, hambre), the complete negation system (nokemo, nokeno, nomodo, nosapa, nulao, falsa, vakua, malu), and deeper communication (opina, poema, signo, arto, storia, fakta, kanto, angla).

49.title: Conversation practice
49.content: Read this dialogue aloud. It uses vocabulary from all seven weeks.
49.aumaText:
A: salama! komo tu senti? mi rongo ke tu esi malu sanita.
B: ya, mi esi lasa e mi senti dolo. mi neda medika.
A: tu debe reposa e beva teo kon miela. miela dona kurita.
B: gratao. mi nokemo senti hambre kwando mi esi malu sanita.
A: mi koka sopa por tu — kon salo e spisao. sabora esi bona.
B: tu esi mui kori. mi kreda ke fortia beni de reposa e kibo bona.
A: klaro. mi skirvi poema por tu: vita esi bona kwando we teni balansa.
B: iti esi bona arto. mi senti plu kalma ya. gratao, ami!
49.englishText:
A: Hello! How do you feel? I hear that you are in bad health.
B: Yes, I am tired and I feel pain. I need medicine.
A: You must rest and drink tea with honey. Honey gives healing.
B: Thanks. I never feel hungry when I am in bad health.
A: I cook soup for you — with salt and spice. The flavor is good.
B: You are very brave-hearted. I believe that strength comes from rest and good food.
A: Of course. I write a poem for you: life is good when we have balance.
B: That is good art. I feel calmer already. Thank you, friend!

49.question: How do you say 'no one can find the island without a sign'?
options: nosapa pude trova isla sen signo
nosapa trova pude isla sen signo
sen signo nosapa pude isla trova
49.explanation: nosapa pude trova isla (no one can find the island) + sen signo (without a sign).

49.question: How do you say 'the forest is far from the city but near the river'?
options: boski esi lonta de sita pero proksima rivo
boski esi lonta sita pero proksima rivo de
lonta de sita boski esi pero proksima rivo
49.explanation: boski esi lonta de sita (forest is far from the city) + pero proksima rivo (but near the river).

49.question: How do you say 'this is a fact: honey is sweet and golden'?
options: iti esi fakta: miela esi suka e dora
fakta iti esi: miela suka e dora esi
iti fakta esi: suka e dora miela esi
49.explanation: iti esi fakta (this is a fact) + miela esi suka e dora (honey is sweet and golden).

50.title: The Wisdom
50.content: You have been learning Auma for fifty days. Now you learn the deepest abstract words — hope (as noun), goodness, beauty, wisdom, knowledge, dream (as noun), the word Auma itself, and ubuntu — the philosophy that 'I am because we are.'

50.sentence: sorao esi en kora di we

50.sentenceTranslation: the beauty of nature is great

50.question: How do you say 'beauty and goodness are in all life'?
options: belina e bonina esi en tota vita
belina bonina esi en tota vita e
en tota vita belina e bonina esi
50.explanation: belina e bonina (beauty and goodness) + esi en tota vita (are in all life).

50.sentenceTranslation: Auma is the language of consciousness
ubuntu is the belief of community

50.title: Philosophy in Auma
50.content: These are the words that live at the intersection of language and consciousness. Auma is not just a language — it is a way of seeing. Ubuntu teaches that we exist through each other.
50.aumaText: mi sabe porke auma esi lingwa di kira: en auma, tota vorto teni signifa · sabina beni de konosia · bonina e belina esi en ubuntu
50.englishText: I know why Auma is the language of consciousness: in Auma, every word has meaning · wisdom comes from knowledge · goodness and beauty are in ubuntu

50.question: How do you say 'wisdom comes from knowledge and time'?
options: sabina beni de konosia e tempo
sabina de konosia e tempo beni
konosia e tempo dona sabina beni
50.explanation: sabina beni de (wisdom comes from) + konosia e tempo (knowledge and time).

50.question: How do you say 'my dream is that we all feel unity'?
options: sonia di mi esi ke we senti unita
mi sonia ke we senti unita esi
sonia mi di esi ke senti we unita
50.explanation: sonia di mi esi ke (my dream is that) + we senti unita (we all feel unity).

51.title: The Mastery
51.content: Your verb vocabulary grows with advanced actions — hide, confirm, verify, restart, celebrate, breathe, practice, and play. These follow the same patterns as all Auma verbs.

51.sentence: mi rekina e mi senti nova
51.sentenceTranslation: I restart and I feel new

51.question: How do you say 'I must confirm and verify the contract'?
options: mi debe konfirma e verifi kontrato
mi debe kontrato konfirma e verifi
konfirma e verifi mi debe kontrato
51.explanation: mi debe (I must) + konfirma e verifi (confirm and verify) + kontrato (the contract).

51.sentenceTranslation: the child plays in the park

51.title: Advanced actions in sentences
51.content: These verbs combine with modals, question words, and discourse markers for sophisticated expression.
51.aumaText: keno tu eskondi iti? · mi devri pratika plu · we selebra kwando we esi hapi · respira, tu pude esi kalma
51.englishText: why do you hide this? · I should practice more · we celebrate when we are happy · breathe, you can be calm

51.question: How do you say 'the child plays and celebrates in the park'?
options: sina juga e selebra en parko
sina selebra e juga en parko
sina en parko juga e selebra
51.explanation: sina (child) + juga e selebra (plays and celebrates) + en parko (in the park).

51.question: How do you say 'breathe deeply and restart'?

52.title: The Measure
52.content: Auma uses the metric system with transparent, recognizable measurement words. These are nouns that follow the same patterns as everything else.

52.sentenceTranslation: the tree is ten meters tall
the market is three kilometers away

52.sentence: mi porta du kilogramo di fruta
52.sentenceTranslation: I carry two kilograms of fruit

52.question: How do you say 'the mountain is two thousand meters tall'?
options: monta esi du mila metro alta
monta alta esi du mila metro
52.explanation: monta esi du mila metro alta (the mountain is two thousand meters tall).

52.sentence: mi beva du litro di akwa
52.sentenceTranslation: I drink two liters of water

52.title: Measurements in conversation
52.content: Use measurements with numbers and di (of) for precise descriptions. The number comes before the unit.
52.aumaText: kwanto kilometro tu kuri? · mi neda un litro di lakto · klima esi fo-des degreo — tro kalda!
52.englishText: how many kilometers do you run? · I need one liter of milk · the temperature is forty degrees — too hot!

52.question: How do you say 'the discount is ten percent'?
How do you ask 'how many liters of water do you need?'
options: kwanto litro di akwa tu neda?
komo litro di akwa tu neda?
kwanto tu neda litro di akwa?
52.explanation: kwanto litro di akwa (how many liters of water) + tu neda (do you need).

53.title: The Bridge
53.content: Foundation gave you e (and), o (or), pero (but), porke (because). Now you learn advanced connectors that build sophisticated arguments and flowing prose.

53.sentence: ademas, mi sabe ke tu esi bona
53.sentenceTranslation: furthermore, I know that you are good
in fact, you are the strongest

53.question: How do you say 'in fact, this is not good'?
53.explanation: enfakta (in fact) + iti no esi bona (this is not good).

53.sentence: tu debe reposa, altramente tu esi lasa
53.sentenceTranslation: you must rest, otherwise you will be tired

53.sentence: mi esi hambre, dono mi kani
53.sentenceTranslation: I am hungry, so I eat

53.title: Building arguments
53.content: Advanced connectors let you build complex arguments, give examples, make concessions, and draw conclusions — all essential for fluent conversation.
53.aumaText: mi ama natura. ademas, natura dona sanita. sinembargo, we debe respeta natura. porekzemplo, we devri respeta arbo. konklusio, we debe respeta natura.
53.englishText: I love nature. Furthermore, nature gives health. However, we must respect nature. For example, we should not cut trees. In conclusion, we should respect nature.

53.question: How do you say 'I read while the child plays'?
53.explanation: mi legi dum sina juga (I read while the child plays).

53.question: How do you say 'you must practice, otherwise you will not know'?
options: tu debe pratika, altramente tu no sabe
tu debe pratika altramente tu no sabe
altramente tu no sabe tu debe pratika
53.explanation: tu debe pratika (you must practice) + altramente (otherwise) + tu no sabe (you will not know).

54.title: The Source
54.content: Auma has a unique feature: evidentiality markers. These small words tell the listener how you know what you are saying — by direct experience, by hearing, by intuition, or by reasoning. This is rare in most languages but natural in Auma.

54.sentenceTranslation: I sense (intuitively) that they are good
I heard that the island is beautiful

54.sentence: padi, tu esi lasa porke tu kuri molta
54.sentenceTranslation: I deduce that you are tired because you run a lot
I see that the sky is blue

54.title: The four ways of knowing
54.content: nuna = I see it (direct). odi = I heard it (hearsay). intu = I feel it (intuition). padi = I deduce it (reasoning). Place these at the start of a sentence to mark your source of knowledge.
54.aumaText: nuna, pluva esi en sielo · odi, merkato teni diskonto · intu, ami di mi esi trisa · padi, tu no domi bona
54.englishText: I see: rain is in the sky · I hear: the market has a discount · I sense: my friend is sad · I deduce: you did not sleep well

54.question: If you heard that there is a festival tomorrow, how do you say it?
54.explanation: odi for hearsay: odi, festa esi doma.

54.sentenceTranslation: I am still reading the book

54.title: Temporal attitude markers
54.content: yari (ready/willing), suda (already), todi (still), and pasuna (was/used to be) add temporal nuance. They tell the listener about timing and completion.
54.aumaText: mi suda trova isla · mi todi serka · mi pasuna en boski · mi esi yari a rekina
54.englishText: I already found the island · I am still searching · I was in the forest · I am ready to restart

54.question: How do you say 'I already know the answer'?
54.explanation: mi suda sabe (I already know) + responda (the answer).

54.question: How do you say 'I deduce that you are still tired'?
54.explanation: padi (I deduce) + tu todi esi lasa (you are still tired).

55.title: The Nuance
55.content: Your quality vocabulary expands with words for weakness, difference, complexity, connection, urgency, lostness, heaviness, and lightness. These add precision to every description.

55.question: How do you say 'we are all different but connected'?
55.explanation: we esi difa pero konekta (we are different but connected).

55.sentenceTranslation: I feel lost in the city

55.title: Nuanced descriptions
55.content: With these words you can describe contrast, urgency, connection, and physical properties. Combine them with comparisons and discourse markers.
55.aumaText: monta esi pesada e petra esi pesada, pero maripi esi levi · mi senti perdita sinembargo mi sabe ke mi esi konekta a komunita di mi · iti esi urjenta e kompla
55.englishText: the mountain is heavy and rock is heavy, but the butterfly is light · I feel lost however I know that I am connected to my community · this is urgent and complex

55.question: How do you say 'the message is urgent, you must read it now'?
options: mesaja esi urjenta, tu debe legi ita nau
mesaja urjenta esi, tu legi debe ita nau
urjenta esi mesaja, nau tu debe legi ita
55.explanation: mesaja esi urjenta (the message is urgent) + tu debe legi ita nau (you must read it now).

55.question: How do you say 'I feel weak but I am not lost'?
options: mi senti debio pero mi no esi perdita
mi senti debio mi no esi perdita pero
debio mi senti pero perdita mi no esi
55.explanation: mi senti debio (I feel weak) + pero mi no esi perdita (but I am not lost).

56.title: The Expansion
56.content: Fifty-six days. Nearly four hundred words. You began Foundation by saying mi esi bona. Now you can ask who, what, where, when, why, and how. You can express ability, obligation, and possibility. You can compare, intensify, and negate with precision. You can talk about time, seasons, health, nature, food, technology, business, art, philosophy, and consciousness itself. You have evidentiality markers and advanced connectors. You have the nuanced vocabulary of a real speaker. The expansion is complete. The depth is in you.

56.title: Extended reading
56.content: Read this passage aloud. It uses vocabulary from all eight weeks of Foundation and Expansion.
56.aumaText: salama, ami. komo tu senti hodi? mi suda skirvi iti en auma porke mi kreda ke auma esi lingwa di kira. enfakta, tota vorto en auma teni signifa. porekzemplo, auma signifa au (spirito) e ma (unita) — spirito en unita, kira konekta. mi pratika auma en han-des-sisa dina. mi sabe kwanto vorto e mi pude konstrui frasa kompla. mi pratika tota dina e mi senti plu inspira. sinembargo, mi sabe ke konosia neva suda — mi debe sempre pratika plu. sabina beni de tempo e pratika. enton, kosa mi sabe nau? mi sabe ke bonina esi en tota vita. mi sabe ke belina esi en natura — en korali di mara, en maripi di boski, en loto di mara. mi sabe ke ubuntu esi bona: mi esi porke we esi. iti esi signifa di auma. konklusio, mi dona gratao a tu, ami — por parla, por rongo, por pratika kon mi. ademas, mi dona promisa: mi sempre pratika. sorao esi en kora di mi. mi esi yari a rekina. tu esi kori e sabina. we esi konekta. adio, ami. salama.
56.englishText: Hello, friend. How do you feel today? I have already written this in Auma because I believe that Auma is the language of consciousness. In fact, every word in Auma has meaning. For example, Auma means au (spirit) and ma (unity) — spirit in unity, consciousness connected. I have practiced Auma for fifty-six days. I know many words and I can build complex sentences. I practice every day and I feel more inspired. However, I know that knowledge is never complete — I must always practice more. Wisdom comes from time and practice. So, what do I know now? I know that goodness is in all life. I know that beauty is in nature — in the coral of the sea, in the butterfly of the forest, in the lotus of the sea. I know that ubuntu is good: I am because we are. This is the meaning of Auma. In conclusion, I give thanks to you, friend — for speaking, for listening, for practicing with me. Furthermore, I give a promise: I will always practice. Hope is in my heart. I am ready to restart. You are courageous and wise. We are connected. Goodbye, friend. Peace.

56.title: Full conversation
56.content: Practice this dialogue. It uses vocabulary from all eight weeks.
56.aumaText:
A: salama! suda molta tempo! komo tu esi?
B: beno, mi esi mui bona, gratao! mi suda pratika auma tota semana.
A: serio? kosa tu pratika iti semana?
B: mi pratika vorto por sanita e natura. porekzemplo, medika, balansa, korali, isla.
A: bona! mi rongo ke tu skirvi poema en auma. ka iti esi bona?
B: ya! enfakta, mi skirvi poema por mama di mi. iti esi sirka belina di verino.
A: mui inspira! ka tu pude legi ita a mi?
B: klaro! intu, iti esi bona poema: belina di verino esi en tota flora. sabina di tana esi en tota vorto. sorao di yuna esi en tota sonia. konklusio, vita esi bona.
A: iti esi mui bona arto. mi senti kori kwando mi rongo ita. ademas, mi kreda ke tu suda teni sabina.
B: gratao, ami. sinembargo, mi todi pratika. sabina neva suda.
A: ubuntu, ami. we pratika porke we esi konekta. adio!
56.englishText:
A: Hello! It has been a long time! How are you?
B: Well, I am very good, thanks! I have been practicing Auma all week.
A: Seriously? What did you practice this week?
B: I practiced words for health and nature. For example, medicine, balance, coral, island.
A: Good! I hear that you write poems in Auma. Is it good?
B: Yes! In fact, I wrote a poem for my mother. It is about the beauty of spring.
A: Very inspiring! Can you read it to me?
B: Of course! I sense it is a good poem: The beauty of spring is in every flower. The wisdom of the elder is in every word. The hope of the young is in every dream. In conclusion, life is good.
A: That is very good art. I feel courageous when I hear it. Furthermore, I believe that you already have wisdom.
B: Thank you, friend. However, I still practice. Wisdom is never complete.
A: Ubuntu, friend. We practice because we are connected. Goodbye!

56.title: Expansion Graduation
56.content: You have completed the Auma Expansion. Eight weeks. Nearly two hundred new words built on the Foundation. You can now hold real conversations, express complex ideas, argue with evidence, and describe the world with nuance. The language is no longer something you study — it is something you speak. The mastery tier awaits.
56.aumaText: gratao, ami. tu esi plu ke pronta — tu esi yari. kira di tu esi klaro. konosia di tu esi granda. kora di tu esi konekta a komunita di we. we senti ubuntu. salama.
56.englishText: Thank you, friend. You are more than ready — you are willing. Your consciousness is clear. Your knowledge is great. Your heart is connected to our community. We feel ubuntu. Peace.

57.title: The Beloved
57.content: You already know mama, papa, sina, sibra, ami, and marito. Today you complete the relationship vocabulary — partners, lovers, exes, and the bonds of the heart. Auma also has three derived forms of ama (love) that express different dimensions of connection.

57.title: The life of bonds
57.content: Relationships change over time. Auma gives you words for the full arc — from partner to spouse to widow to ex.

57.translation: children / child group (regular plural of sina + -li)

57.title: The three faces of love
57.content: Auma derives three forms from ama (love): amala is unconditional love, amara is romantic love, amana is communal love. The suffix changes the type of love expressed.
57.englishText: unconditional love · romantic love · communal love

57.sentenceTranslation: mother feels unconditional love for children
I feel romantic love for you

57.question: How do you say 'my beloved is calm'?
57.explanation: alohi di mi (my beloved) + esi (is) + kalma (calm).

57.question: Which word means 'romantic love'?
57.explanation: amara is romantic love, amala is unconditional, amana is communal.

57.question: How do you say 'the widow is strong'?
How do you say 'our children are happy'?
57.explanation: sinali di we (our children, inclusive) + esi hapi (are happy).

58.title: The Modifier
58.content: Today you learn the small words that change meaning dramatically. Modifiers, conjunctions, and particles — these are the glue of fluent speech. Each one reshapes how a sentence feels.

58.title: Direction of time
58.content: abi and ven mark direction in time. abi points to a habitual or ongoing action, ven points to something about to happen.
58.aumaText: mi abi kani → mi ven kani
58.englishText: I have been eating → I am about to eat

58.title: Passive voice with -ita
58.content: To say something was done to something without naming who did it, add the passive suffix -ita after a hyphen. The root never changes: kani becomes kani-ita, skirvi becomes skirvi-ita, koka becomes koka-ita, and lava becomes lava-ita. The pattern is: object + esi + verb-root-ita. The hyphen keeps the suffix visually separate from ita = that.
58.aumaText: pano esi kani-ita · libro esi skirvi-ita · sopa esi koka-ita · tomo esi lava-ita
58.englishText: the bread is eaten · the book is written · the soup is cooked · the house is cleaned

58.title: The AUMA tense system
58.content: AUMA marks time with particles placed BEFORE the verb. You now know te (just happened) and ven (about to happen). The full system has four markers: te (just), ven (about to), pasa (past — taught Day 74), and fura (future — taught Day 81). Without any particle, AUMA sentences are present tense by default. With context words like yesta or doma, the time is clear. The particles add precision: mi pasa kani = I ate. mi fura kani = I will eat.

58.title: Connecting ideas
58.content: You already know e (and), o (or), pero (but), and de (from/of). Now add asta (until), tala (such/so that), and koma (like/as).

58.question: How do you say 'I only want water'?
58.explanation: solo (only) modifies the subject: mi solo voli akwa.

58.question: Which word means 'about to / going to'?
58.explanation: ven points forward in time — about to happen.

58.question: How do you say 'you are like a star'?
58.explanation: koma (like/as) connects the comparison: tu esi koma stela.

58.question: How do you say 'I will practice until evening'?
58.explanation: mi ven pratika (I will practice) + asta sera (until evening).

59.title: The Harvest
59.content: You already know akwa, pano, keso, salo, suka, kopi, teo, and sopa. Today you fill out the pantry with fruits, grains, and staples from around the world.

59.title: The global kitchen
59.content: Auma borrows food words transparently from world languages. You can already guess many of them.

59.sentence: pesao kon risao esi kibo di sera

59.question: How do you say 'I want to drink cocoa'?
59.explanation: mi voli (I want) + beva (to drink) + kokoa (cocoa).

59.question: How do you say 'rice with vegetables is good'?
59.explanation: risao kon legu (rice with vegetables) + esi bona (is good).

59.question: Which word means 'fish (food)'?
59.explanation: pesao is fish as food; pesa is the living fish.

59.question: How do you say 'mango is my fruit'?
59.explanation: mango esi (mango is) + frutao di mi (my fruit).

60.title: The Temple
60.content: You already know korpo, kapo, oki, boka, mano, kora. Today you name the body in full — from hair to toes. Your body is a temple. Name every part of it.

60.title: Inside the mouth
60.content: The mouth (boka) contains the tongue (linga), the teeth (denti), and the stomach (stoma) processes what enters.

60.sentence: linga esi por parla e kani
60.sentenceTranslation: the tongue is for speaking and eating

60.title: Hands and feet
60.content: You know mano (hand). Now learn dita (finger), gamba (leg), and peda (foot) — the limbs that move you through the world.

60.sentence: mi teni des dita en mano
60.sentenceTranslation: I have ten fingers on my hands

60.question: How do you say 'my hair is good'?
60.explanation: peli di mi (my hair) + esi bona (is good).

60.question: Which word means 'shoulder'?
60.explanation: spala is shoulder, stoma is stomach, dorso is back.

60.question: How do you say 'my back feels pain'?
60.explanation: dorso di mi (my back) + senti (feels) + dolo (pain).

60.question: How do you say 'the tongue is for speaking and eating'?
options: linga esi por parla e kani
linga por parla esi e kani
por parla e kani linga esi
60.explanation: linga esi (the tongue is) + por parla e kani (for speaking and eating).

61.title: The Sacred Place
61.content: You know tomo (house), parko, kafe, merkato, ofisa, templo. Today you learn places sacred and domestic — shrines, villages, beaches, and rooms inside the home.

61.sentenceTranslation: the shrine is on the mountain
the field is green and big
I want to go to the beach

61.title: Settlement and community
61.content: From the smallest village to the largest city — Auma names where people gather.

61.sentence: vila di mi esi pika pero bona
61.sentenceTranslation: my village is small but good

61.sentence: pola esi inter vila e sita
61.sentenceTranslation: a town is between a village and a city

61.title: Rooms of the home
61.content: Every home has rooms. Learn the spaces inside your tomo (house).

61.sentenceTranslation: the roof of the house is red

61.question: How do you say 'the children go to school'?
Which word means 'shrine / sanctuary'?
61.explanation: sanatano is a shrine/sanctuary, lerotano is a school, templo is a temple.

61.question: How do you say 'I cook in the kitchen'?
61.explanation: mi koka (I cook) + en kosina (in the kitchen).

61.question: How do you say 'the field is green and big'?
61.explanation: kampo esi (the field is) + verda e granda (green and big).

62.title: The Cry
62.content: Every language has sounds that bypass grammar — exclamations of surprise, pain, joy, and warning. Today you learn the interjections of Auma, plus three discourse words that anchor what you say in honesty.

62.sentence: opa! libro di mi no esi en tomo
62.sentenceTranslation: oops! my book is not at home

62.title: Quick sounds
62.content: Some interjections are just a breath — he for calling attention, uf for frustration, hm for thinking, sh for silence.

62.title: Discourse anchors
62.content: Three words that frame your speech with authenticity — basika (basically), enverda (truly), honesta (honestly).

62.sentenceTranslation: basically, I want to practice more
truly, you are a good friend
honestly, I do not know the answer

62.question: Which interjection means 'ouch / pain'?
62.explanation: aya expresses pain, opa is oops, uf is frustration.

62.question: How do you say 'shh! the baby is sleeping'?
62.explanation: sh! (quiet) + nuri esi domi (the baby is sleeping).

62.question: Which word means 'truly / in truth'?
62.explanation: enverda means truly/in truth, honesta means honestly, basika means basically.

62.question: How do you say 'hey! listen to me'?
62.explanation: he! (hey) + rongo a mi (listen to me).

63.title: The Reflection
63.content: Week 9 gave you the full relationship vocabulary, modifiers that reshape meaning, the complete pantry, the entire body, sacred and domestic places, and the sounds of spontaneous expression. You have deepened from grammar into life.

63.title: Reading passage
63.content: Read this passage aloud. It uses vocabulary from Week 9 and all previous tiers.
63.aumaText: salama, alohi. hodi mi ven parla de vita di we. novio di mi e mi esi en vila pika. we teni gardi kon flora e arbo. matina, mi koka en kosina — risao kon legu e buta. sinali kani banana e mango. tali esi hapi. sera, we esi en sala e parla. mi senti amala por sinali di we. honesta, vita en kampo esi koma sonia. dorso di mi senti dolo de pratika pero kora di mi esi bona. fin, mi sabe ke amana esi plu granda ke tomo — iti esi komunita.
63.englishText: Hello, beloved. Today I will speak about our life. My partner and I are in a small village. We have a garden with flowers and trees. In the morning, I cook in the kitchen — rice with vegetables and butter. The children eat banana and mango. They are happy. In the evening, we sit in the living room and talk. I feel unconditional love for our children. Honestly, life in the countryside is like a dream. My back feels pain from work but my heart is good. Finally, I know that communal love is bigger than a house — it is community.

63.question: What does 'amala' mean?
63.explanation: amala is unconditional love, amara is romantic, amana is communal.

63.question: How do you say 'I am about to sleep'?
63.explanation: ven marks something about to happen: mi ven domi.

63.question: Which word means 'kitchen'?
63.explanation: kosina is kitchen, sala is living room, bano is bathroom.

63.question: How do you say 'my nose is small'?
63.explanation: naso di mi (my nose) + esi pika (is small).

63.question: Which interjection expresses surprise/amazement?
63.explanation: wa means wow/amazing, uf is frustration, hm is thinking.

63.question: How do you say 'both friends are good'?
What does 'basika' mean?
63.explanation: basika means basically, honesta means honestly, enverda means truly.

64.title: The Craft I
64.content: You already know many verbs — anda, kuri, nada, salta, flugi, lava, koka. Today begins a three-day verb intensive. These are verbs of skill, creation, and consequence.

64.sentenceTranslation: you photograph flowers in the garden
the elder guides the children to school

64.title: Verbs of action and change
64.content: kesi means to stop or cease. kina means to move or stir. kona means to know through experience. lero means to learn.

64.sentence: mi kona dolo e mi kona hapi
64.sentenceTranslation: I know pain and I know happiness
put the book on the bed

64.question: How do you say 'I film our celebration'?
64.explanation: mi filma (I film) + festa di we (our celebration).

64.question: Which word means 'stop / cease'?
64.explanation: kesi is stop, kina is move, kona is know by experience.

64.question: How do you say 'put the book on the bed'?
64.explanation: pona (put) + libro (book) + en kama (on the bed).

64.question: How do you say 'they win the game'?

65.title: The Craft II
65.content: Today you learn verbs of becoming and transformation — crying, mixing, being born, leaving, and the deepest actions of consciousness.

65.sentence: nuri laka porke ta esi hambre
65.sentenceTranslation: the baby cries because they are hungry
the baby is born in the morning

65.title: Going and being
65.content: parti means to leave or depart. pali means to speak or say. mori is to die. medita is to meditate — the verb of inner stillness.

65.question: How do you say 'the baby cries because they are hungry'?
options: nuri laka porke ta esi hambre
laka nuri porke hambre ta esi
65.explanation: nuri laka (baby cries) + porke (because) + ta esi hambre (they are hungry).

65.question: Which word means 'be born'?
65.explanation: nasi is be born, mori is die, parti is leave.

65.question: How do you say 'mix fruit with milk'?
How do you say 'I meditate every morning'?
65.explanation: mi medita (I meditate) + tota matina (every morning).

66.title: The Craft III
66.content: The final verb batch — precise actions: sitting, cutting, separating, uniting. These complete your active vocabulary.

66.sentenceTranslation: sit in the living room and rest
I cut bread with my hand

66.title: Time, sound, and union
66.content: timi means to time or measure duration. sora means to sound or ring. uni means to unite or join. vesti means to dress or wear.

66.question: How do you say 'I separate good from bad'?
66.explanation: mi separi (I separate) + bona de malu (good from bad).

66.question: Which word means 'unite / join'?
66.explanation: uni is unite, separi is separate, miksa is mix.

66.question: How do you say 'music sounds in the temple'?
66.explanation: musika sora (music sounds) + en templo (in the temple).

66.question: How do you say 'sit in the living room and rest'?
66.explanation: seda (sit) + en sala (in the living room) + e reposa (and rest).

67.title: The Network
67.content: You know komputa, telefona, reta, skirmo, tekla, klika, senda, resiva, karga, konto. Today you go deeper into digital life — apps, data, social media, and the infrastructure of connection.

67.sentenceTranslation: the phone battery is small / low

67.title: Media and access
67.content: video, foto, wifai, kargador — the tools of modern communication.

67.sentenceTranslation: I film a video for social media
I need a charger for my phone
I send a link to you
I do not want social media today
I upload a photo to the network

67.question: How do you say 'the phone battery is small / low'?
67.explanation: bateria di telefona (phone battery) + esi pika (is small / low). The more precise word basa is taught later.

67.question: Which word means 'download / upload'?
67.explanation: subi is download/upload, senda is send, karga is charge/load.

67.question: How do you say 'I send a link to you'?
67.explanation: mi senda (I send) + linka (a link) + a tu (to you).

67.question: How do you say 'is the WiFi good here?'?
67.explanation: ka (question) + wifai esi bona (WiFi is good) + hiro (here).

68.title: The Bestiary
68.content: You know anima, avi, pesa, maripi. Today you meet the creatures and habitats of Auma — dolphins, cats, dogs, snakes, and the nests and lakes they call home.

68.sentenceTranslation: the dolphin swims in the sea

68.sentence: feli domi en kama di mi
68.sentenceTranslation: the cat sleeps on my bed

68.title: Reptiles and birds
68.content: tortu is turtle, serpa is snake, rana is frog, korvo is crow. These creatures complete the Auma bestiary.

68.sentenceTranslation: the turtle is slow but wise
the snake is in the grass
the frog jumps in the lake
the crow is black and good

68.title: Habitats and growth
68.content: Every creature needs a home. nido is nest, koli is hill, fola is leaf, herba is grass, wata is water (as element), lako is lake.

68.sentenceTranslation: the bird builds a nest in the tree
the leaf of the tree is green
the grass is green in the field
the water of the lake is clear
the lake is serene in the morning

68.question: How do you say 'the dolphin swims in the sea'?
68.explanation: delfina nada (the dolphin swims) + en mara (in the sea).

68.question: Which word means 'nest'?
68.explanation: nido is nest, koli is hill, fola is leaf.

68.question: How do you say 'the frog jumps in the lake'?
68.explanation: rana salta (the frog jumps) + en lako (in the lake).

68.question: How do you say 'the cat sleeps on my bed'?
options: feli domi en kama di mi
feli en kama domi di mi
domi feli en kama di mi
68.explanation: feli domi (the cat sleeps) + en kama di mi (on my bed).

69.title: The Measure
69.content: You know bona, malu, nova, kalda, frida. Today you learn the physical dimensions — tall and short, wide and narrow, big and small. These are the qualities of space and scale.

69.title: Size review
69.content: You learned granda (big) and pika (small) in Week 1. Now we use them with precise measurement: alta (tall), basa (short/low), kurta (short length), larga (wide).

69.title: Length and width
69.content: longa is long, kurta is short (length). larga is wide, streta is narrow. These describe objects, paths, and spaces.

69.sentenceTranslation: the road is narrow in the village

69.title: Age, speed, and strength
69.content: anti is old (opposite of nova). veka is ancient/elderly. rapi is fast/quick. fortao is strong (as quality noun).

69.sentenceTranslation: the tree is ancient and big
the dolphin is fast in the sea

69.question: How do you say 'the mountain is tall'?
How do you say 'the tree is ancient and big'?
69.explanation: arbo esi (the tree is) + veka e granda (ancient and big).

69.question: How do you say 'the road is narrow in the village'?
69.explanation: via esi streta (the road is narrow) + en vila (in the village).

70.title: The Reflection
70.content: Week 10 gave you twenty-four new verbs of skill and consequence, the full digital vocabulary, the creatures and habitats of Auma, and the dimensions of the physical world. You can now describe what you do, what you see, and the shape of everything around you.

70.title: Reading passage
70.content: Read this passage aloud. It uses vocabulary from Week 10 and all previous tiers.
70.aumaText: hodi mi parti de sita granda e mi anda a lako. via esi longa e streta pero mi kina rapida. kwando mi beni a lako, mi nuna ke wata esi klaro e sereni. delfina nada en mara lonta pero hiro rana salta en herba. feli di mi seda en petra e rongo avi en arbo. mi fota tota — foto di fola verda, di korvo negra, di nido en arbo alta. mi subi foto a sosia pero pos mi bloka sosia porke mi voli reposa. mi seda en herba, mi rongo sora di wata, e mi medita. basika, natura esi plu bona ke reta.
70.englishText: Today I leave the big city and I go to the lake. The road is long and narrow but I move quickly. When I come to the lake, I see that the water is clear and serene. Dolphins swim in the distant sea but here frogs jump in the grass. My cat sits on a rock and listens to birds in the tree. I photograph everything — photos of green leaves, of black crows, of nests in tall trees. I upload photos to social media but then I block social media because I want to rest. I sit in the grass, I listen to the sound of water, and I meditate. Basically, nature is better than the internet.

70.question: Which word means 'photograph / take a photo'?
70.explanation: fota is the verb (to photograph), filma is to film, foto is the noun (photo).

70.question: How do you say 'I move my body'?
70.explanation: mi kina (I move) + korpo di mi (my body).

70.question: Which word means 'lake'?
70.explanation: lako is lake, rivo is river, mara is sea.

70.question: How do you say 'the bird builds a nest in the tree'?
70.explanation: avi konstrui (the bird builds) + nido (a nest) + en arbo (in the tree).

70.question: What does 'rapi' mean?
70.explanation: rapi means fast/quick, lenta means slow.

70.question: How do you say 'I need a charger for my phone'?
70.explanation: mi neda (I need) + kargador (charger) + por telefona (for phone).

70.question: How do you say 'we unite for harmony'?
70.explanation: we uni (we unite) + por armoni (for harmony).

71.title: The Deep
71.content: You have learned the body, the world, the creatures, and the verbs. Now you enter the abstract — the concepts behind consciousness itself. Today: condition, change, mind, doubt, growth, death, birth, and three untranslatable concepts from world philosophy.

71.sentenceTranslation: change is the nature of life

71.sentence: mente di mi esi klaro hodi

71.title: Life and death
71.content: nasita is birth, morta is death, kresao is growth. These name the arc of all existence. dubio is doubt — the beginning of wisdom.

71.sentence: dubio esi en mente di mi

71.sentenceTranslation: the birth of a baby is a celebration
death is part of the circle

71.title: The untranslatable
71.content: sirkulo is the circle — the shape of all cycles. ikigai is borrowed from Japanese philosophy — it names the intersection of what you love, what you're good at, what the world needs, and what sustains you. wabi comes from the Japanese aesthetic tradition wabi-sabi — it honors the beauty found in imperfection, incompleteness, and impermanence. These words are borrowed with respect, preserving their original nuance.

71.sentence: vita esi sirkulo de nasita e morta
71.sentenceTranslation: life is a circle of birth and death

71.sentence: ikigai di mi esi lero e parla auma
71.sentenceTranslation: my life purpose is to learn and speak Auma

71.sentence: wabi esi en tota kosa anti
71.sentenceTranslation: beauty of imperfection is in every old thing

71.question: Which word means 'life purpose / reason for being'?
71.explanation: ikigai is life purpose, wabi is beauty of imperfection, sirkulo is circle.

71.question: How do you say 'change is the nature of life'?
71.explanation: kambio esi (change is) + natura di vita (the nature of life).

71.question: How do you say 'death is part of the circle'?
71.explanation: morta esi (death is) + parti de sirkulo (part of the circle).

72.title: The Spectrum
72.content: You know hapi, trisa, sereni, ira, kalma, sola, inspira, kreati, foka, distra, kontenta, frustra, amora, kori, sabar. Today you complete the emotional palette — bravery, boredom, humility, anxiety, despair, and more.

72.sentence: ta esi brani en tota kosa

72.sentenceTranslation: the elder is humble and wise

72.title: Pride, doubt, and gratitude
72.content: proda is proud, dubi is doubtful, grata is grateful. These three shape how we see ourselves and others.

72.sentenceTranslation: mother is proud of the children

72.sentence: mi esi dubi de deside di mi
72.sentenceTranslation: I am doubtful of my decision

72.sentence: ta senti vako pos morta di ami
72.sentenceTranslation: they feel emptiness after a friend dies
the children are bored at school

72.sentence: no esi despa — spera esi hiro
72.sentenceTranslation: do not despair — hope is here

72.sentence: mente di mi esi klara nau
mi esi konfu — mi no sabe kemo
72.sentenceTranslation: I am confused — I do not know why

72.question: Which word means 'humble'?
72.explanation: humi is humble, brani is brave, proda is proud.

72.question: How do you say 'I am grateful for life'?
72.explanation: mi esi grata (I am grateful) + por vita (for life).

72.question: Which word means 'anxious / nervous'?
72.explanation: ansi is anxious, despa is desperate, konfu is confused.

72.question: How do you say 'you are strong and brave'?
72.explanation: tu esi (you are) + forta e brani (strong and brave).

73.title: The Texture
73.content: You know qualities of size, temperature, and weight. Today you learn textures and abstract qualities — clean and dark, soft and hard, simple and difficult, full and few.

73.sentenceTranslation: the night is dark and calm

73.title: Truth and ease
73.content: vera is true/real, simpla is simple, fasila is easy, difisa is difficult. These describe the nature of ideas and tasks.

73.title: Fullness and value
73.content: belao is beauty (as quality), kara is dear/expensive, klarao is clarity, moda is wet, plena is full, poka is few/little.

73.sentence: alohi esi plu kara ke dora
73.sentenceTranslation: the beloved is more precious than gold
the grass is wet from rain

73.question: How do you say 'the water is clean'?
How do you say 'the beloved is more precious than gold'?
options: alohi esi plu kara ke dora
alohi plu kara esi ke dora
plu kara alohi esi ke dora
73.explanation: alohi esi (the beloved is) + plu kara ke dora (more precious than gold).

73.question: What does 'plena' mean?
73.explanation: plena is full, poka is few, limpa is clean.

74.title: The Clock
74.content: You know tempo, hodi, doma, yesta, nau, sempre, neva. Today you master temporal precision — brief and long, first and final, often and rarely, and the particles that pin actions to exact moments.

74.sentence: iti esi fina vorto di mi

74.title: Frequency
74.content: ofta is often, rara is rarely, vesa is sometimes. These tell how frequently something happens.

74.sentenceTranslation: I often meditate in the morning

74.title: Moment markers
74.content: justa means just now. pronto means soon. ankora means still/yet/again. na is a present-moment particle. nu means now (emphatic). sona means to sleep or slumber.

74.question: How do you say 'life is brief'?
Which word means 'often / frequently'?
74.explanation: ofta is often, rara is rarely, vesa is sometimes.

74.question: How do you say 'I am still learning Auma'?
74.explanation: mi ankora lero (I still learn) + auma.

74.question: Which word means 'first'?
74.explanation: prima is first, fina is final, breva is brief.

75.title: The Village
75.content: Today you learn the vocabulary of community and the remaining emotions. Truth, respect, culture, tradition — the words that bind society. Plus the final emotional shadings: evil, pain, risk, sacred, thirsty, alive, and weak.

75.sentence: verita esi plu forta ke falsa
sorio di tu dona hapi a mi
75.sentenceTranslation: your smile gives happiness to me

75.title: Culture and cooperation
75.content: paso is peace/step, kultura is culture, tradisa is tradition, gotong is mutual cooperation (from gotong-royong).

75.sentenceTranslation: the first step is always difficult
mutual cooperation is the heart of the village

75.title: The final emotions
75.content: mala is evil/wicked, peno is sorrow/grief, riska is risky/daring, santa is sacred/holy, sega is thirsty, sura is angry (sustained), viva is alive/vibrant, debi is weak (emotionally).

75.sentenceTranslation: they are not evil — they are confused

75.sentence: mi esi sega — dona akwa plisa
75.sentenceTranslation: I am thirsty — give water please

75.sentence: ta esi sura pero mi esi kalma
75.sentenceTranslation: they are furious but I am calm

75.sentence: mi senti debi pero mi no esi perdita
75.sentenceTranslation: I feel weak but I am not lost

75.question: How do you say 'truth is stronger than falsehood'?
options: verita esi plu forta ke falsa
verita plu forta esi ke falsa
plu forta verita esi ke falsa
75.explanation: verita esi (truth is) + plu forta ke falsa (stronger than falsehood).

75.question: Which word means 'mutual cooperation'?
75.explanation: gotong is mutual cooperation, tradisa is tradition, kultura is culture.

75.question: How do you say 'I feel alive in nature'?
75.explanation: mi senti (I feel) + viva (alive) + en natura (in nature).

75.question: Which word means 'sacred / holy'?
75.explanation: santa is sacred, mala is evil, sura is furious.

76.title: The Precision
76.content: Auma has a unique error taxonomy — seven specific words for different types of errors. This is the language of debugging, correction, and precision. You also learn two evidentiality markers that complete the source system.

76.sentence: mi teni erospeka — mi pensa iti esi difa
76.sentenceTranslation: I had the wrong expectation — I thought it was different

76.sentence: erokatena — paso du beni ante paso un
76.sentenceTranslation: sequence error — step two came before step one

76.sentence: mi teni erointu — mi senti pero no esi vera
76.sentenceTranslation: my intuition was wrong — I felt it but it was not true

76.title: System errors
76.content: eroporta means a transport/delivery error. erovalida means invalid input. eropermisa means a permission error. erosalama means a safety violation.

76.sentence: eroporta — mesaja no beni a tu
76.sentenceTranslation: delivery error — the message did not reach you
invalid — the data is not good
permission denied — you do not have permission

76.sentence: erosalama — iti esi no sega por sinali
76.sentenceTranslation: safety violation — this is not safe for children

76.title: Evidentiality completions
76.content: You already know nuna (by sight), odi (by hearing), intu (by intuition), padi (by reasoning). Now add nevidi (not witnessed) and nontenta (not attempted/untried).

76.sentence: iti esi nevidi — nosapa nuna iti
76.sentenceTranslation: this is unseen — no one saw it

76.sentence: iti esi nontenta — we debe proba
76.sentenceTranslation: this is untried — we must try

76.question: Which error type means 'wrong expectation'?
76.explanation: erospeka is wrong expectation, erointu is wrong intuition, erokatena is broken chain.

76.question: How do you say 'permission denied'?
76.explanation: eropermisa is permission denied, erosalama is safety violation, eroporta is delivery failed.

76.question: What does 'nevidi' mean?
76.explanation: nevidi is unseen/not witnessed, nontenta is untried, erovalida is invalid.

76.question: How do you say 'the message did not reach you'?
76.explanation: mesaja no beni (the message did not come) + a tu (to you).

77.title: The Reflection
77.content: Week 11 took you into the depths — love, change, mind, doubt, the full emotional palette, textures and qualities, temporal mastery, community vocabulary, and the error taxonomy. You can now express the most abstract ideas in Auma with precision.

77.title: Reading passage
77.content: Read this passage aloud. It uses vocabulary from Week 11 and all previous tiers.
77.aumaText: mi senti viva hodi. na mi seda en gardi e mi pensa de sirkulo di vita. prima, nasita — nuri laka e rongo e lero. pos, kresao — mi lero de dubio, de peno, de pasi. mi ofta senti konfu pero ankora mi lero. vesa mi senti debi pero mi sabe ke kambio dona fortao. mi justa rongo musika de templo e mi senti klarao en mente. ikigai di mi esi klaro: mi voli lero, mi voli parla verita, mi voli uni kon komunita. tradisa di we esi santa. gotong esi kora di kultura di we. honesta, mi esi grata. vita esi breva pero plena. enverda, mi ama vita e mi ama tota vorto di auma.
77.englishText: I feel alive today. Right now I sit in the garden and I think about the circle of life. First, birth — the baby cries and listens and learns. Then, growth — I learn from doubt, from grief, from passion. I often feel confused but still I learn. Sometimes I feel weak but I know that change gives strength. I just heard music from the temple and I feel clarity in my mind. My life purpose is clear: I want to learn, I want to speak truth, I want to unite with community. Our tradition is sacred. Mutual cooperation is the heart of our culture. Honestly, I am grateful. Life is brief but full. Truly, I love life and I love every word of Auma.

77.question: What does 'ikigai' mean?
77.explanation: ikigai is life purpose, wabi is beauty of imperfection, sirkulo is circle.

77.question: How do you say 'I rarely feel angry'?
77.explanation: mi rara senti (I rarely feel) + ira (angry).

77.question: Which word means 'clean / pure'?
77.explanation: limpa is clean, mola is soft, moda is wet.

77.question: How do you say 'change is difficult but necessary'?
77.explanation: kambio esi difisa (change is difficult) + pero neda (but necessary).

77.question: What does 'erokatena' mean?
77.explanation: erokatena is a sequence error (broken chain).

77.question: How do you say 'the first step is always difficult'?
77.explanation: paso prima (first step) + esi sempre difisa (is always difficult).

77.question: Which word means 'grief / sorrow'?
77.explanation: peno is grief, ansi is anxious, despa is desperate.

78.title: The Action
78.content: Today brings the remaining verbs of power — opening, closing, counting, destroying — plus qualities of wealth, sameness, and quantity. These complete your active vocabulary.

78.title: Diminish and destroy
78.content: minu means to reduce or diminish. tero means to erase or destroy. tupa means to hit or strike. perda means to lose.

78.sentence: no tero libro — lero de ita
78.sentenceTranslation: do not destroy the book — learn from it
knock on the house and ask

78.title: Qualities of wealth and quantity
78.content: pova is poor, riko is rich, brata is cheap/free, sama is same, solao is solo/alone (quality), susa is dry, tota is every/all, molta is many/much.

78.sentence: pova en konto pero riko en kora
78.sentenceTranslation: poor in account but rich in heart
water is free in the village

78.sentence: mi esi solao pero no esi sola
78.sentenceTranslation: I am alone but not lonely
the land is dry in summer

78.question: How do you say 'open the book and read'?
Which word means 'reduce / diminish'?
78.explanation: minu is reduce, tero is erase/destroy, perda is lose.

78.question: How do you say 'we are all the same'?
78.explanation: we tota (we all) + esi sama (are the same).

78.question: How do you say 'I count stars at night'?
78.explanation: mi konta (I count) + stela (stars) + en noka (at night).

79.title: The Elements
79.content: You know tera, mara, sielo, pluva, klima. Today you complete the natural world — fire, wind, sun, clouds, and weather. These are the primal forces.

79.sentenceTranslation: the wind moves leaves from the tree
the sun gives light to the earth
clouds are white in the sky

79.title: Life in detail
79.content: radi is root, risa is laughter, sanda is sand, spisa is spice, vala is valley, kosta is coast.

79.sentence: radi di arbo esi en tera
79.sentenceTranslation: the root of the tree is in the earth
laughter is medicine of the heart
the sand of the beach is hot
the valley is between two mountains

79.sentence: vila di mi esi en kosta
79.sentenceTranslation: my village is on the coast

79.title: Weather phenomena
79.content: arkoiris is rainbow, humida is humid, nebla is fog, snova is snow, torma is storm.

79.sentenceTranslation: the evening is humid in summer

79.sentence: nebla esi en vala en matina
79.sentenceTranslation: fog is in the valley in the morning
snow is white on the mountain
the storm comes from the sea

79.question: How do you say 'fire is hot and red'?
79.explanation: fira esi (fire is) + kalda e roja (hot and red).

79.question: Which word means 'rainbow'?
79.explanation: arkoiris is rainbow, nebla is fog, snova is snow.

79.question: How do you say 'the valley is between two mountains'?
79.explanation: vala esi (the valley is) + inter du monta (between two mountains).

79.question: How do you say 'laughter is medicine of the heart'?
79.explanation: risa esi (laughter is) + medika di kora (medicine of the heart).

80.title: The Life Cycle
80.content: Today brings the remaining abstract concepts, body parts, and all remaining colors. Fear, energy, justice, spirit — plus the final body vocabulary and the full color spectrum.

80.sentence: timio esi natura pero brani esi kambio
80.sentenceTranslation: fear is natural but bravery is the change
the energy of the sun gives life

80.sentence: justia esi por tota — no solo por riko
80.sentenceTranslation: justice is for everyone — not only for the rich

80.title: The remaining body
80.content: aleria is allergy/reaction, brasa is arm, menta is chin, osi is bone, sanga is blood, peto is chest.

80.sentenceTranslation: I have an allergy to nuts

80.sentence: brasa di mi esi forta de pratika
80.sentenceTranslation: my arms are strong from practice

80.sentence: osi esi dura pero pude rompi
80.sentenceTranslation: bone is hard but can break
blood gives life to the body

80.title: The remaining colors
80.content: You know blanka, negra, roja, blu, verda, yela, purpura, dora. Now learn the remaining seven colors.

80.sentenceTranslation: the evening is orange and red
pink flowers are in the garden
the moon is silver at night
the sea is turquoise at the beach

80.question: How do you say 'justice is for everyone'?
80.explanation: justia esi (justice is) + por tota (for everyone).

80.question: Which word means 'bone'?
80.explanation: osi is bone, brasa is arm, sanga is blood.

80.question: How do you say 'the moon is silver at night'?
80.explanation: luna esi silva (the moon is silver) + en noka (at night).

80.question: Which word means 'turquoise / teal'?
80.explanation: turki is turquoise, gri is gray, bruna is brown.

81.title: The Calendar
81.content: You know the days of the week (dina-un through dina-seti) and basic time words. Today you complete the calendar — times of day, months of the year, and two more temporal markers.

81.sentence: laborina esi de dina-un a dina-han
81.sentenceTranslation: workdays are from Monday to Friday

81.title: Time transitions
81.content: fura is future/ahead. agon is ago/past. despues is after/then.

81.sentence: molta anua agon mi nasi en vila
81.sentenceTranslation: many years ago I was born in a village

81.title: The twelve months
81.content: Auma months follow the same number logic as days. mensa-un is January (month one), mensa-du is February, and so on through mensa-des-du (December).
81.aumaText: mensa-un · mensa-du · mensa-tri · mensa-fo · mensa-han · mensa-sisa · mensa-seti · mensa-soka · mensa-suna · mensa-des · mensa-des-un · mensa-des-du
81.englishText: January · February · March · April · May · June · July · August · September · October · November · December

81.question: What is 'July' in Auma?
81.explanation: July is the 7th month, so mensa-seti (month-seven).

81.question: How do you say 'I eat at noon'?
81.explanation: mi kani (I eat) + en medidina (at noon).

81.question: Which word means 'weekend'?
81.explanation: findina is weekend, laborina is workday, posmedi is afternoon.

81.question: How do you say 'after rain, the rainbow comes'?
81.explanation: despues pluva (after rain) + arkoiris beni (the rainbow comes).

82.title: The Raw
82.content: Every living language has words for frustration, disgust, taboo, and raw emotional pressure. Today you learn Auma's hard swear words — not nerfed, not childish — plus the mathematical operations that represent the precision end of the spectrum.

82.title: Cultural note on swearing
82.content: Auma treats hard profanity as emotional release and intensity, not as hate. You may say fuck, shit, damn, or call out cruel behavior, but Auma does not canonize identity slurs, dehumanizing attacks, or punching down. The rule is simple: pressure-release is allowed; hate is not.

82.sentence: damu! mi perda konto di mi
feka! mi perda konto di mi
emosio di mi esi kruda hodi

82.sentenceTranslation: the asshole does not respect the friend

82.title: Mathematics
82.content: From the crude to the precise — the five mathematical operations. These are verbs in Auma.

82.question: Which word is the hard Auma equivalent of 'fuck'?
82.explanation: feka is the hard fuck/fucking expletive. damu is damn, and skata is shit/crap.

82.question: How do you say 'two plus two equals four'?
82.explanation: du adita du (two plus two) + egala fo (equals four).

82.question: Which word means 'divide'?
82.explanation: divida is divide, multa is multiply, subtra is subtract.

82.question: Which word means 'asshole / jerk / cruel fool'?
82.explanation: asola is the personal-insult word. It should be used rarely and never as hate language.

82.question: What is the cultural rule about swearing in Auma?
options: Pressure-release is allowed; hate and identity slurs are not
Use insults as often as possible
82.explanation: Auma allows hard profanity as pressure-release and intensity, but rejects identity slurs, hate language, and dehumanizing attacks.

83.title: The Completion
83.content: This is the final teaching day. Every remaining canonical word enters your vocabulary today — pronouns, numbers, places, directions, and technology. These are the last pieces of the complete 750-word Auma canon.

83.title: Advanced pronouns
83.content: You know mi, tu, ta, mili, tuli, tali, we. Now learn the demonstratives, reflexives, and special forms.
83.aumaText: ano · anoli · iti · ita · itili · itali · siri · uno-uno · weto
83.englishText: that one · those ones · this · that · these · those · self/reflexive · each other · together

83.sentenceTranslation: these are flowers from the garden
those are stars in the sky

83.title: Advanced numbers
83.content: You know nula through sento. Now learn the higher numbers, fractions, and ordinals.
83.aumaText: uno · mila · milona · bilona · trilona · medi · kuarta · punta · un-imo · du-imo · des-imo
83.englishText: one (standalone) · thousand · million · billion · trillion · half · quarter · point/dot · first · second · tenth

83.sentence: medi di pano esi por tu
83.sentenceTranslation: half of the bread is for you

83.sentence: kuarta di hori esi des-han minuta
83.sentenceTranslation: a quarter of an hour is fifteen minutes

83.title: The tens (compound numbers)
83.content: You learned the tens pattern in Day 2. These compound forms are now formally registered.
83.aumaText: du-des · tri-des · fo-des · han-des · sisa-des · seti-des · soka-des · suna-des

83.title: Remaining places
83.content: The last domestic and spatial places in the canon.
83.aumaText: fena · muro · plasa · ponta · pora · resta · suloa · denta · eksta · senta · infra · hiro · tiro
83.englishText: window · wall · plaza · bridge · door · restaurant · floor · inside · outside · center · below · here · there

83.sentenceTranslation: open the window — the wind comes

83.title: Compound directions
83.content: You know nori, sudi, esti, westi. The diagonals combine them.

83.title: Remaining technology
83.content: The final digital vocabulary — system operations and media tools.
83.aumaText: mesia · nulo · parta · pasvordo · prosa · eskalti · aborti · delega · audita
83.englishText: media · null/empty · share/part · password · process · escalate · abort · delegate · audit

83.sentenceTranslation: do not give the password to nobody

83.question: How do you say 'I know myself'?
What is 'July' expressed as a month number?
How do you say 'the bridge unites two villages'?
83.explanation: ponta uni (the bridge unites) + du vila (two villages).

83.question: Which word means 'here'?
83.explanation: hiro is here, tiro is there, denta is inside.

84.title: The Graduation
84.content: Eighty-four days. Over six hundred words. You began by saying mi esi bona. Now you can speak about love and death, justice and fear, the body and the cosmos, technology and tradition, error and precision. You can count to a trillion, name every month, describe every color, and express every emotion. You have learned three types of love, seven types of errors, four evidentiality markers, and the core grammar of a living language. You are not finished — no real speaker ever is — but you are no longer outside the language. You can enter it, use it, and keep growing inside it.

84.title: The final reading
84.content: Read this aloud. It is yours.
